exam_tip: Idle cut-off shuts off fuel for a controlled shutdown. It is preferred over switching off magnetos because it clears fuel from the induction system, making an inadvertent start less likely.
key_facts: Stops all fuel flow to the engine
Used for controlled engine shutdown
Clears fuel from the induction system (reduces fire risk)
Preferred over switching off magnetos
source: ATC Aircraft General Knowledge (Ch. 4: idle cut-off), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.12.6(e)

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the idle cut-off control
concept: The idle cut-off control (also called the fuel selector or mixture control in some contexts) allows the pilot to stop fuel flow to the engine for engine shutdown. Moving the control to the idle cut-off position closes a fuel valve, stopping all fuel delivery to the engine. This causes the engine to cease combustion and stop running. The idle cut-off control is used before shutdown and also in emergency situations (e.g., engine fire) to stop fuel flow immediately. Some aircraft use a dedicated fuel selector valve for this function; others integrate it into the main fuel system control.
exam_tip: Idle cut-off stops fuel flow to shut down the engine. Used before normal shutdown and in emergencies to stop fuel delivery.
key_facts: Stops fuel flow to the engine
Used before engine shutdown
Can be used in emergencies (e.g. engine fire)
Causes engine to stop running
May be part of fuel selector valve or separate control
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (Fuel systems), ATC Aircraft General Knowledge, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.12.6

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the manual mixture control and idle cut-off
concept: The mixture control adjusts the fuel-to-air ratio by moving a needle valve in the carburettor. Two primary functions: (1) Power management — at altitude, the pilot leans the mixture to maintain the correct fuel/air ratio as air density decreases, optimising fuel burn and engine performance; (2) Engine shutdown — moving the mixture to the idle cut-off position cuts fuel flow, starving the engine for a controlled shutdown. The mixture control has a range from full rich (maximum fuel) through various lean positions to idle cut-off (no fuel).
exam_tip: Mixture control does two jobs: adjust the fuel/air ratio for altitude (lean as you climb), and shut down the engine (idle cut-off). Full rich for takeoff and low altitude; progressively leaner as altitude increases.
key_facts: Mixture control: adjusts fuel-to-air ratio via needle valve
Full rich for takeoff and low altitude
Lean progressively as altitude increases
Idle cut-off: stops all fuel for engine shutdown
Range: full rich → lean → idle cut-off
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (mixture control), ATC AGK (Ch. 4), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.12.8

syllabus_desc: Explain the correct operational use of a manual mixture control and idle cut-off
concept: The mixture control adjusts the fuel-to-air ratio. At sea level, the engine runs best with a mixture near stoichiometric (approximately 15 parts air to 1 part fuel by weight). As altitude increases, the air becomes thinner, so the pilot must progressively lean the mixture to maintain the correct fuel-to-air ratio. Failure to lean at altitude results in an over-rich mixture. For engine shutdown, the mixture is moved to the idle cut-off position, which starves the engine of fuel for a controlled shutdown.
exam_tip: As altitude increases, lean the mixture to maintain the correct ratio. The correct shutdown procedure is mixture to idle cut-off (not magnetos off first).
key_facts: Mixture adjusts fuel-to-air ratio
Stoichiometric ratio: approximately 15:1 air to fuel by weight
Lean the mixture as altitude increases
Idle cut-off position for engine shutdown
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (mixture control at altitude), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.12.10

syllabus_desc: Explain the consequences of operating with over-rich mixture settings
concept: An over-rich mixture means excess fuel that cannot be completely burned. Consequences include: reduced power output (incomplete combustion), increased fuel consumption (fuel is wasted), possible spark plug fouling (carbon deposits from unburned fuel accumulate on the spark plugs), black exhaust smoke, and lower cylinder head temperatures (excess fuel has a cooling effect). Prolonged operation with an excessively rich mixture wastes fuel and degrades engine performance.
exam_tip: Over-rich = excess fuel = incomplete combustion = fouled plugs, wasted fuel, reduced power. Note that CHT tends to be lower (not higher) with a rich mixture because the excess fuel absorbs heat.
key_facts: Reduced power output (incomplete combustion)
Increased fuel consumption
Spark plug fouling (carbon deposits)
Black exhaust smoke
Lower cylinder head temperatures
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (rich mixture effects, CHT discussion), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.12.12(a)

syllabus_desc: Explain the consequences of operating with over-lean mixture settings
concept: An over-lean mixture means insufficient fuel relative to the air entering the engine. Consequences include: engine overheating (insufficient fuel for cooling effect), increased risk of detonation and pre-ignition, rough running and loss of power, potential engine damage from excessively high cylinder head temperatures, and in extreme cases, engine failure. Lean mixtures are particularly dangerous at high power settings where the engine is already running hot.
exam_tip: Over-lean = not enough fuel = overheating, detonation risk, rough running, potential engine damage. Especially dangerous at high power settings.
key_facts: Engine overheating (insufficient fuel cooling)
Increased risk of detonation and pre-ignition
Rough running and loss of power
High cylinder head temperatures
Potential engine damage or failure at extreme lean settings
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (lean mixture effects), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.12.12(b)

syllabus_desc: Describe detonation and pre-ignition and distinguish between them
concept: Detonation is the uncontrolled, explosive burning of the end gases in the combustion chamber after the spark plug has fired. Instead of a smooth flame front, the remaining mixture detonates, creating sharp pressure spikes that can crack pistons, damage cylinder heads, and destroy the engine. Pre-ignition is the ignition of the fuel/air mixture before the spark plug fires, caused by a hot spot in the combustion chamber (such as a carbon deposit or overheated spark plug). Both cause engine damage but through different mechanisms — detonation occurs after the spark, pre-ignition occurs before it.
exam_tip: Detonation = after the spark (end gases explode). Pre-ignition = before the spark (hot spot ignites the mixture). Both are bad, but they are different phenomena with different causes.
key_facts: Detonation: explosive burning of end gases AFTER the spark
Pre-ignition: mixture ignites BEFORE the spark (from a hot spot)
Detonation causes sharp pressure spikes, piston/head damage
Pre-ignition caused by carbon deposits, overheated plugs
Both cause severe engine damage
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (detonation and pre-ignition sections), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.12.14

syllabus_desc: State the causes of detonation and pre-ignition and the avoidance measures
concept: Detonation causes: using fuel with lower octane than specified, operating at high power with lean mixture (insufficient fuel cooling), high engine temperatures, excessively advanced ignition timing. Pre-ignition causes: hot carbon deposits in the combustion chamber, overheated spark plugs, sharp edges or protrusions in the combustion chamber. Avoidance: use the correct fuel grade (AVGAS 100LL), maintain a suitably rich mixture at high power settings, monitor CHT and reduce power if temperatures are excessive, avoid prolonged operation at high power with lean mixture, and maintain the engine to prevent carbon buildup.
exam_tip: Detonation: wrong fuel, lean mixture at high power, high temperatures. Pre-ignition: hot spots from carbon deposits. Avoid both by using correct fuel, enriching at high power, and monitoring CHT.
key_facts: Detonation: low octane fuel, lean at high power, high temps, advanced timing
Pre-ignition: hot carbon deposits, overheated plugs
Use correct fuel grade (AVGAS 100LL)
Enrich mixture at high power settings for cooling
Monitor CHT; reduce power if excessive
Regular maintenance to prevent carbon buildup
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (detonation/pre-ignition causes and avoidance), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.12.16

syllabus_desc: Describe the three types of carburettor ice: refrigeration, throttle, and impact ice
concept: Refrigeration ice forms as fuel evaporates in the carburettor, absorbing heat and cooling the surrounding surfaces. In humid conditions (even at temperatures as high as 20°C), water vapour in the air can freeze on carburettor walls. Throttle ice forms specifically on the throttle butterfly valve and the carburettor throat walls due to the pressure and temperature drop as air passes the partially closed throttle. Impact ice forms when visible moisture (cloud droplets, rain, or snow) freezes directly on the exterior of the carburettor air intake and filter, similar to structural icing.
exam_tip: Three types: refrigeration (fuel evaporation cools surfaces), throttle (pressure drop at butterfly valve), impact (visible moisture freezes on intake). Refrigeration ice is the most insidious — it can form on a clear day at 20°C.
key_facts: Refrigeration ice: fuel evaporation cools surfaces; forms even at high ambient temps
Throttle ice: pressure/temperature drop at butterfly valve
Impact ice: visible moisture freezes on air intake exterior
Refrigeration ice most common and insidious (forms at 0-20°C in humid air)
Throttle ice most common at low power (throttle partially closed)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (carburettor ice types), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.12.18

syllabus_desc: Describe the conditions favourable to carburettor ice formation
concept: Carburettor ice can form when: the ambient temperature is between approximately -5°C and +25°C (most likely between 0°C and 20°C), humidity is moderate to high, and the engine is operating at low to medium power (throttle partially closed, maximising the cooling effect). The fuel evaporation in the carburettor can cause a temperature drop of up to 30°C, meaning ice can form even on warm, clear days. Descending flight at low power in humid conditions is a particularly high-risk situation.
exam_tip: Carb ice can form on a warm, clear day — this catches students out. The danger zone is 0-20°C with moderate humidity, especially at low power. Descending flight is high risk.
key_facts: Temperature range: -5°C to +25°C (most likely 0-20°C)
Moderate to high humidity
Low to medium power settings (throttle partially closed)
Fuel evaporation can cause up to 30°C temperature drop
Descending flight at low power is high risk
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (carb ice conditions), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.12.20(a)

syllabus_desc: Describe the use of the carburettor heat gauge
concept: Some aircraft have a carburettor air temperature (CAT) gauge that indicates the temperature of the air entering the carburettor. This helps the pilot determine whether conditions are favourable for carburettor icing. If the CAT is in the icing range (typically indicated by a yellow arc on the gauge), the pilot should apply carburettor heat pre-emptively. The gauge provides early warning before ice actually forms, allowing preventive action rather than reactive correction.
exam_tip: The CAT gauge shows carburettor air temperature. If it's in the yellow arc (icing range), apply carb heat. It's a preventive tool — don't wait for symptoms.
key_facts: CAT gauge: indicates carburettor air temperature
Yellow arc on gauge indicates icing range
Apply carb heat pre-emptively when in icing range
Provides early warning before ice forms
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (CAT gauge), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.12.20(b)

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms of carburettor ice and the appropriate corrective action
concept: Symptoms of carburettor ice include: unexplained loss of power without any throttle change, rough engine running, RPM or manifold pressure dropping with a fixed throttle setting, and the engine possibly stopping if ice is severe. Corrective action: apply full carburettor heat immediately. Expect a temporary drop in RPM when heat is first applied (because warmer air is less dense, momentarily enriching the mixture). If ice was present, RPM should rise as the ice melts and normal airflow is restored. If conditions remain conducive to icing, leave carburettor heat on.
exam_tip: Apply full carb heat — not partial. Expect an initial RPM drop, then a rise as ice clears. The temporary RPM drop is normal and expected. If you don't get a rise afterwards, ice may be too severe — descend to warmer air.
key_facts: Symptoms: unexplained power loss, rough running, RPM drop
Action: apply FULL carburettor heat immediately
Expect temporary RPM drop (warmer, less dense air)
RPM should recover as ice melts
If no recovery, descend to warmer air
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (carburettor ice symptoms), Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (carb heat procedure), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.12.20(c)

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the inlet manifold
concept: The inlet manifold (intake manifold) is a set of tubes that distribute the fuel/air mixture from the carburettor (or throttle body) to each cylinder. It must be designed to provide equal mixture distribution to all cylinders for even running. The manifold also provides a smooth internal surface to minimise fuel condensation and ensure efficient mixture flow. Manifold pressure (MAP) is measured in the inlet manifold and indicates the pressure of the mixture entering the cylinders — it is used as a primary power setting indicator on aircraft with constant-speed propellers.
exam_tip: Inlet manifold distributes the fuel/air mixture to all cylinders. Manifold pressure (MAP) is measured here and indicates engine power setting.
key_facts: Inlet manifold: distributes fuel/air mixture to each cylinder
Must provide equal distribution for even running
Smooth internal surface to minimise fuel condensation
Manifold pressure (MAP) measured here as power indicator
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (inlet manifold), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.12.22

syllabus_desc: State the advantages and disadvantages of fuel injection compared to carburettor systems
concept: Advantages of fuel injection: more uniform fuel distribution to each cylinder (each has its own injector), no carburettor icing risk (no venturi for ice to form on), better mixture control, improved fuel efficiency, and better cold-start characteristics. Disadvantages: more complex and expensive to maintain, harder to detect vapour lock (fuel vaporises in the fuel lines on hot days, disrupting fuel flow), and hot-start difficulties on some fuel-injected engines (fuel vaporises in the lines when engine is hot). Note: while carburettor icing is eliminated, the air intake can still ice over.
exam_tip: Advantages: better fuel distribution, no carb icing, better efficiency. Disadvantages: more complex, vapour lock risk on hot days, hot-start difficulties. Remember: intake icing is still possible.
key_facts: Advantage: more uniform fuel distribution to each cylinder
Advantage: no carburettor icing risk
Advantage: better mixture control and fuel efficiency
Disadvantage: more complex and expensive maintenance
Disadvantage: vapour lock risk on hot days
Disadvantage: hot-start difficulties
Note: air intake icing still possible
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (injection vs carburettor), Module 1.7 (vapour lock), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.14.2

syllabus_desc: Explain the function and principles of a fuel injection system
concept: A fuel injection system meters and delivers fuel directly to each cylinder, bypassing the carburettor entirely. This provides more uniform fuel distribution across all cylinders compared to a carburettor system (where some cylinders may run richer or leaner than others). Fuel injection offers better mixture control, reduced risk of vapour lock compared to carburettors, improved cold-start characteristics, and is immune to carburettor icing (though the engine air intake can still ice over).
exam_tip: The key advantage of fuel injection is more uniform fuel distribution to each cylinder. It eliminates the carburettor (and therefore carburettor icing) but does not eliminate all icing risks — the air intake can still ice over.
key_facts: Delivers fuel directly to each cylinder
More uniform fuel distribution than a carburettor
No carburettor icing risk (but intake icing still possible)
Better mixture control and cold-start characteristics
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (carburettor vs direct injection), Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (fuel injection and carb ice immunity), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.14.4(a)

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between direct and indirect fuel injection
concept: Direct injection delivers fuel directly into the combustion chamber of each cylinder. Indirect injection (port injection) delivers fuel into the intake port just upstream of the inlet valve, where it mixes with incoming air before entering the cylinder. Most light aircraft use indirect (port) injection via a continuous-flow system. Direct injection is more common in automotive engines and some newer aviation diesel engines. Indirect injection is simpler and adequate for the operating conditions of most light aircraft engines.
exam_tip: Most light aircraft use indirect (port) injection — fuel is sprayed into the intake port, not directly into the cylinder. Direct injection sprays into the combustion chamber itself.
key_facts: Direct injection: fuel delivered directly into combustion chamber
Indirect (port) injection: fuel delivered into intake port upstream of valve
Most light aircraft use indirect/port injection
Direct injection more common in automotive and diesel aviation engines
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (injection types), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.14.4(b)

syllabus_desc: Explain the operating principle of a simple fuel injection system
concept: In a continuous-flow fuel injection system (common in Lycoming and Continental engines), fuel flows continuously through individual nozzles at each cylinder's intake port. An engine-driven fuel pump pressurises fuel from the tanks. A fuel control unit meters the flow rate based on throttle position and mixture setting. The metered fuel is distributed to individual nozzle lines, one per cylinder, providing even fuel delivery. Unlike a carburettor where fuel is atomised in a venturi, the fuel injection system sprays fuel directly at each cylinder's intake.
exam_tip: Continuous-flow means fuel is always flowing through the nozzles — it is not pulsed or timed to individual strokes. The fuel control unit meters the flow; individual nozzles distribute it evenly.
key_facts: Continuous-flow system: fuel flows constantly through nozzles
Engine-driven fuel pump pressurises fuel
Fuel control unit meters flow based on throttle and mixture
Individual nozzle at each cylinder's intake port
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (fuel injection principles), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.14.6

syllabus_desc: State the purpose of the fuel delivery pump system in fuel injection
concept: The engine-driven fuel pump draws fuel from the tanks and delivers it at regulated pressure to the fuel metering unit (fuel control unit). The pump maintains consistent fuel pressure regardless of engine speed or altitude changes. Some aircraft also have an electric boost pump (auxiliary pump) that can be switched on manually during takeoff and landing for additional fuel pressure assurance, and to assist with engine starting.
exam_tip: The fuel delivery pump's job is to supply fuel at a regulated, consistent pressure to the fuel metering unit. The boost pump provides backup pressure and assists during critical phases of flight.
key_facts: Engine-driven pump delivers fuel at regulated pressure
Supplies fuel to the fuel metering/control unit
Maintains consistent pressure regardless of conditions
Electric boost pump provides backup during takeoff/landing
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (fuel pump and boost pump), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.14.8(a)

syllabus_desc: Describe the fuel distribution system in fuel injection
concept: The fuel distribution system takes metered fuel from the fuel control unit and divides it equally among individual fuel lines leading to each cylinder's injection nozzle. A fuel distributor (or flow divider) ensures equal fuel flow to all lines regardless of slight differences in nozzle back-pressure. Equal distribution is critical — uneven fuel delivery would cause some cylinders to run rich and others lean, resulting in rough running, uneven temperatures, and potential engine damage.
exam_tip: The fuel distributor divides metered fuel equally to all cylinder nozzles. Equal distribution is critical for smooth, even engine operation.
key_facts: Fuel distributor divides metered fuel equally to all nozzle lines
One fuel line per cylinder
Equal distribution prevents uneven running
Unequal distribution causes rough running and uneven CHT
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (fuel distribution), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.14.8(b)

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of fuel injectors
concept: Fuel injectors (nozzles) are precision-calibrated orifices located at each cylinder's intake port. They spray a fine mist of fuel into the intake air just upstream of the inlet valve. The nozzle orifice size is carefully calibrated to deliver the correct fuel quantity at the operating pressure. The fine spray promotes good atomisation and mixing with the intake air. Blocked or partially blocked nozzles result in lean running on the affected cylinder, causing rough running and potentially high CHT on that cylinder.
exam_tip: Injectors spray a fine fuel mist into each cylinder's intake port. They are precision-calibrated — a blocked nozzle means that cylinder runs lean.
key_facts: Precision-calibrated nozzles at each cylinder intake port
Spray fine fuel mist for good atomisation
Calibrated orifice size controls fuel delivery
Blocked nozzle: lean running on that cylinder, rough engine
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (injector function), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.14.8(c)

syllabus_desc: Identify common aviation fuel types and their colour identification
concept: AVGAS 100LL (Low Lead) is dyed blue and is the standard fuel for most piston-engine aircraft. AVGAS 100 (higher lead content) is dyed green but is rarely available. AVTUR (Jet A-1) is clear to straw-coloured and is used in turbine (jet) engines — it must never be used in piston engines. Fuel colour identification is a critical safety check during refuelling to ensure the correct fuel type is being loaded. Using the wrong fuel can cause immediate engine failure.
exam_tip: AVGAS 100LL = BLUE. AVGAS 100 = GREEN. AVTUR (jet fuel) = CLEAR/STRAW. Always check fuel colour during refuelling. Wrong fuel = engine failure.
key_facts: AVGAS 100LL: dyed BLUE (standard for piston engines)
AVGAS 100: dyed GREEN (rarely available)
AVTUR (Jet A-1): clear to straw-coloured (turbine engines only)
Always check fuel colour during refuelling
Wrong fuel type can cause immediate engine failure
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (fuel types and colours), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.16.2

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between the characteristics of AVGAS, MOGAS, and AVTUR
concept: AVGAS 100LL: 100 octane anti-detonation rating, contains tetraethyl lead as an anti-knock agent, designed for high-performance piston engines, relatively low volatility. MOGAS (automotive gasoline): lower octane rating (typically 91-95), not approved for aircraft without a specific STC, higher volatility (vapour lock risk), may contain ethanol which damages fuel system components. AVTUR (Jet A-1): kerosene-based turbine fuel, completely unsuitable for piston engines, much lower volatility than AVGAS, designed for jet engines operating at altitude and extreme cold.
exam_tip: AVGAS: 100 octane, leaded, for piston aircraft. MOGAS: lower octane, volatile, needs STC. AVTUR: kerosene, for jets only — never in a piston engine.
key_facts: AVGAS 100LL: 100 octane, contains lead, for piston engines
MOGAS: lower octane, higher volatility, needs STC for aircraft use
AVTUR: kerosene-based, for turbine engines only
Never use AVTUR in a piston engine
Never use MOGAS without STC approval
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (fuel characteristics), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.16.4

syllabus_desc: State the precautions regarding the use of MOGAS in aero-engines
concept: MOGAS (motor gasoline) may only be used in an aero-engine if the aircraft has a specific Supplemental Type Certificate (STC) approving its use for that aircraft type. Key precautions include: higher risk of vapour lock in hot weather (MOGAS has higher vapour pressure than AVGAS), the octane rating must be correct for the engine, MOGAS may contain ethanol which can damage fuel system components, and fuel shelf life is shorter than AVGAS. MOGAS should not be mixed with AVGAS.
exam_tip: The critical requirement is an STC — without one, MOGAS cannot be used. The biggest operational risk is vapour lock in hot conditions.
key_facts: Requires a Supplemental Type Certificate (STC) for the aircraft
Higher vapour lock risk in hot weather
Correct octane rating must be verified
May contain ethanol (damaging to some fuel system components)
Shorter shelf life than AVGAS; do not mix with AVGAS
source: ATC Aircraft General Knowledge (Glossary: MOGAS definition), Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (fuel types), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.16.6

syllabus_desc: State common fuel contaminants and the precautions to avoid them
concept: The most common fuel contaminant is water, which enters through condensation in partly-filled fuel tanks (especially overnight when air cools) or from contaminated fuel supplies. Other contaminants include dirt, dust, metal debris, and microbial growth. Precautions: fill fuel tanks completely after the last flight of the day (reduces air space and condensation), ensure fuel caps are clean and sealed tightly, perform a fuel drain check before every flight by drawing fuel from the lowest point of each tank and the fuel strainer, check that fuel is clear or slightly hazy (brown colour or visible droplets indicate water contamination — aircraft is unserviceable until drained and cleaned).
exam_tip: Water is the most common contaminant. Fill tanks after flying to reduce condensation. Always drain and check fuel samples before flight. Brown or cloudy fuel = water contamination = do not fly.
key_facts: Most common contaminant: water (condensation)
Fill tanks after last flight to minimise condensation
Check fuel caps are clean and sealed
Drain fuel samples from tank sumps and strainer before flight
Clear/slightly hazy fuel = acceptable
Brown or visible water droplets = unserviceable
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (fuel contamination), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.16.8

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the exhaust manifold
concept: The exhaust manifold collects burned gases from all cylinders and directs them through a single exhaust pipe to be expelled overboard. It is designed to minimise backpressure (which would reduce engine efficiency) and allow efficient scavenging of exhaust gases from the cylinders. In many light aircraft, the exhaust manifold also serves as a heat source for the cabin heating system and for carburettor heat — exhaust gases warm a heat shroud, and air passing over this shroud is directed into the cabin or carburettor.
exam_tip: The exhaust manifold collects burned gases from all cylinders and vents them overboard. It also provides heat for cabin heating and carburettor heat on many aircraft.
key_facts: Collects exhaust gases from all cylinders
Directs gases overboard through single exhaust pipe
Designed to minimise backpressure
Often provides heat source for cabin heating and carb heat
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (exhaust manifold), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.18.2

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of exhaust manifold sealing
concept: Proper sealing of the exhaust manifold is critical for two reasons: first, exhaust gas leaks can damage nearby engine components and wiring through exposure to extremely hot gases; second, and more critically, a leak in the exhaust manifold (especially where it passes through the cabin heating shroud) can allow carbon monoxide (CO) to enter the cabin. Since CO is colourless and odourless, even a small leak can be extremely dangerous. Exhaust system integrity must be inspected regularly during maintenance.
exam_tip: Exhaust leaks are dangerous because they can let carbon monoxide into the cabin — CO is colourless and odourless. Exhaust integrity must be checked regularly.
key_facts: Exhaust leaks can damage nearby components
Leaks can allow carbon monoxide (CO) into the cabin
CO is colourless and odourless — extremely dangerous
Especially critical near cabin heating shroud
Regular inspection of exhaust integrity required
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (exhaust sealing), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.18.4

syllabus_desc: Describe the sources, indications, and dangers of carbon monoxide
concept: Carbon monoxide (CO) is a colourless, odourless, toxic gas produced as a by-product of combustion. Sources in aircraft include exhaust system leaks (especially through cabin heating shrouds that use exhaust heat), cracked exhaust manifolds, and poorly sealed engine compartments. CO binds to haemoglobin in the blood 200-250 times more readily than oxygen, rapidly reducing the blood's ability to carry oxygen. Symptoms include headache, dizziness, nausea, drowsiness, confusion, and loss of consciousness. Some aircraft have CO detector patches that change colour in the presence of CO. If CO is suspected: ventilate the cabin immediately (open air vents and windows), turn off cabin heat, land as soon as practicable, and seek medical attention.
exam_tip: CO is colourless and odourless — you can't detect it without a CO detector. Symptoms: headache, dizziness, drowsiness. If suspected: open vents, turn off cabin heat, land ASAP.
key_facts: CO: colourless, odourless, toxic gas from combustion
Binds to haemoglobin 200-250× more readily than oxygen
Sources: exhaust leaks, especially through cabin heat shroud
Symptoms: headache, dizziness, nausea, drowsiness, confusion
Action: ventilate cabin, turn off cabin heat, land ASAP
CO detector patches change colour as warning
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (carbon monoxide), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.18.6

syllabus_desc: Describe the principal features of a magneto ignition system (dual, independent, engine-driven)
concept: Light aircraft engines use dual magneto ignition: two separate, independent magneto systems each supply one spark plug per cylinder, so each cylinder has two spark plugs. Each magneto is mechanically driven by the engine (via the crankshaft), is self-contained, and operates independently of the aircraft electrical system. This provides redundancy — if one magneto fails, the engine continues running on the other. Having two spark plugs per cylinder also improves combustion efficiency (two simultaneous flame fronts burn the mixture more completely and quickly).
exam_tip: Key points: magnetos are engine-driven (not electrically powered), independent of the aircraft electrical system, and the dual system provides both redundancy and better combustion. The engine will run on one magneto — just with slightly reduced power.
key_facts: Dual magnetos: two independent systems
Each magneto powers one spark plug per cylinder (two plugs per cylinder)
Mechanically driven by the engine crankshaft
Independent of the aircraft electrical system
Provides redundancy and improved combustion efficiency
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2, Module 1.7, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.20.2

syllabus_desc: State the purpose and principle of an impulse coupling
concept: An impulse coupling is a mechanical device fitted to one (sometimes both) magnetos that serves two purposes during engine starting: it retards the spark timing (preventing kickback — where the engine fires before TDC and tries to rotate backwards), and it provides a sharp snap rotation to the magneto (producing a hotter spark at the low cranking speeds typical during starting). Once the engine is running at normal RPM, the impulse coupling disengages and the magneto operates normally.
exam_tip: Two purposes: retard timing (prevent kickback) and provide a hotter spark (at low cranking speed). It only operates during starting — once the engine is running, it disengages.
key_facts: Retards spark timing during starting (prevents kickback)
Provides a hotter spark at low cranking speeds
Mechanical device fitted to the magneto
Disengages once the engine reaches normal operating RPM
source: ATC Aircraft General Knowledge (Glossary and Ch. 15: impulse coupling functions), Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.20.4

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of ignition/starter switches and the starter warning light
concept: The ignition switch typically has positions: OFF, RIGHT (right magneto only), LEFT (left magneto only), BOTH (both magnetos), and START (spring-loaded — engages the starter motor and may retard ignition timing). In normal flight, the switch must be on BOTH. The start position is spring-loaded so it returns to BOTH once released. A starter warning light illuminates while the starter motor is engaged — if it remains lit after engine start, the starter has not disengaged and may overheat or be damaged. The pilot should shut down and investigate if the light stays on.
exam_tip: Normal flight = BOTH. Start position is spring-loaded. If the starter warning light stays on after start, the starter hasn't disengaged — shut down immediately.
key_facts: Positions: OFF, RIGHT, LEFT, BOTH, START
Normal operation: BOTH (both magnetos active)
START: spring-loaded, returns to BOTH when released
Starter warning light: illuminates during cranking
If light stays on after start: starter not disengaged — shut down
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (ignition switches), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.20.6

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose and typical procedure for conducting magneto checks
concept: The magneto check (mag check) verifies that both magneto systems are functioning correctly before takeoff. Procedure: set the throttle to the RPM specified in the aircraft manual (typically around 1,000–1,200 RPM). Switch the ignition from BOTH to LEFT and note the RPM drop. Return to BOTH and let the engine stabilise. Switch to RIGHT and note the RPM drop. Return to BOTH. Expected results: a small RPM drop on each magneto (typically not exceeding 150 RPM) because the engine is running on only one spark plug per cylinder. The differential between the two drops should also not exceed a specified limit. No drop indicates a fault (the dead magneto may have been grounded and is now live), and an excessive drop indicates a magneto or spark plug problem.
exam_tip: A small RPM drop is normal and expected (one plug per cylinder = slightly less efficient combustion). No drop is actually a problem — it could mean one magneto was not grounding properly. Excessive drop indicates a fault.
key_facts: Purpose: verify both magneto systems work before takeoff
Switch from BOTH to LEFT, note RPM drop, return to BOTH
Switch from BOTH to RIGHT, note RPM drop, return to BOTH
Expected: small RPM drop on each (typically <150 RPM)
No drop = potential fault; excessive drop = magneto/plug problem
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2, Module 1.7, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.20.8

syllabus_desc: State the procedures and precautions for hand-swinging a propeller
concept: Hand-swinging (hand-propping) is starting an engine by manually rotating the propeller. It is now rare but may be required on some heritage or small aircraft without electric starters. Critical precautions: ensure the area behind and around the propeller is clear of people and obstructions; only trained personnel should attempt it; throttle must be set to idle; brakes must be set or chocks in place; a qualified person must be at the controls; treat every propeller as live — never stand in the plane of rotation; use a sharp downward flick of the blade and step back immediately. The magnetos should be off until ready to start to prevent accidental firing.
exam_tip: Treat every propeller as if it could fire at any time. Only trained people should hand-swing. Brakes set, throttle idle, someone at the controls. Step clear immediately after the swing.
key_facts: Only trained personnel should attempt hand-swinging
Throttle at idle, brakes set or chocks in place
Qualified person must be at the controls
Clear area around propeller before swinging
Magnetos off until ready to start
Step clear immediately after swinging blade downward
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (hand-swinging), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.20.10

syllabus_desc: Describe the features and components of a solid state ignition system
concept: Solid state (electronic) ignition systems use electronic circuits and sensors to generate and time the ignition spark, replacing the mechanical breaker points used in magneto systems. Components typically include: an electronic control unit (ECU), magnetic or Hall-effect sensors for crankshaft position detection, ignition coils, and spark plugs. Some systems have dual electronic channels for redundancy. They are increasingly used in modern light aircraft engines.
exam_tip: Solid state = electronic ignition. No mechanical breaker points. Uses sensors and an ECU to time the spark. More reliable, less maintenance than magnetos.
key_facts: Electronic circuits replace mechanical breaker points
Components: ECU, crankshaft position sensors, ignition coils, spark plugs
Some systems have dual channels for redundancy
No contact points to wear or burn
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (solid state ignition), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.22.2

syllabus_desc: State the advantages and disadvantages of solid state ignition
concept: Advantages: increased reliability (no mechanical parts to wear), reduced maintenance, more consistent spark timing across all conditions, better cold-start characteristics, and potentially improved fuel efficiency from optimised timing. Disadvantages: typically dependent on the aircraft electrical system (unlike magnetos which are self-powered), more complex electronics requiring specialist repair, and potentially more expensive initial installation.
exam_tip: Main advantage: more reliable, less maintenance. Main disadvantage: may depend on aircraft electrical system (unlike self-powered magnetos). Check which system your aircraft has.
key_facts: Advantage: increased reliability (no wear parts)
Advantage: reduced maintenance
Advantage: more consistent spark timing
Advantage: better cold-start characteristics
Disadvantage: may depend on aircraft electrical system
Disadvantage: requires specialist electronic repair
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (solid state advantages), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.22.4

syllabus_desc: Describe the purpose and procedure for an ignition integrity check on solid state systems
concept: The ignition integrity check verifies that the electronic ignition system is functioning correctly before flight. Since solid state systems have no visible moving parts, the check is primarily operational: verify the engine starts reliably and runs smoothly during the run-up, check that RPM response is normal when testing each ignition channel individually (similar to a magneto check), and verify that any warning lights or fault indicators are not illuminated. Some systems have built-in test (BIT) functions that can be activated from the cockpit.
exam_tip: Similar to a mag check but for electronic channels. Check each channel individually for RPM drop. Look for warning lights or fault indicators.
key_facts: Verify engine starts reliably and runs smoothly
Check each ignition channel individually (like mag check)
Verify normal RPM response on each channel
Check that no warning lights or fault indicators are illuminated
Some systems have built-in test (BIT) functions
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (ignition checks), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.22.6

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of the ignition/starter switch for solid state systems
concept: The ignition/starter switch for solid state systems operates similarly to the magneto ignition switch, with positions for OFF, individual channel selection (Channel 1, Channel 2), BOTH channels, and START. The START position engages the starter motor. Normal operation is on BOTH channels. The switch may also incorporate a key-operated lock for security. Some solid state systems use a separate electronic ignition master switch in addition to the standard ignition switch.
exam_tip: Same basic operation as magneto switch: OFF, individual channels, BOTH, START. Normal flight = BOTH channels active.
key_facts: Switch positions: OFF, Channel 1, Channel 2, BOTH, START
Normal operation: BOTH channels active
START position engages starter motor
May include key-operated lock for security
Some systems use separate electronic ignition master switch
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (ignition switch operation), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.22.8

syllabus_desc: State the safety precautions for engine starting
concept: Before starting: clear the area — ensure no people or obstructions near the propeller; set parking brake or use chocks; verify fuel quantity and selector position; check mixture set to rich (at low altitude); throttle at idle; master switch ON; confirm magnetos/ignition as required for start sequence. Call 'CLEAR PROP' loudly before engaging the starter. During starting: limit continuous cranking to 10-15 seconds to avoid overheating the starter motor. After starting: check oil pressure rises to the green arc within 30 seconds; if no oil pressure indication, shut down immediately.
exam_tip: Always call 'CLEAR PROP' before starting. Check oil pressure immediately after start — if no pressure within 30 seconds, shut down.
key_facts: Clear area near propeller before starting
Set parking brake; verify fuel and mixture
Call 'CLEAR PROP' loudly
Limit continuous cranking to 10-15 seconds
Check oil pressure rises to green arc within 30 seconds
No oil pressure = shut down immediately
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (starting safety), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.24.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedure for starting a cold engine
concept: To start a cold engine: (1) Set fuel selector to ON and verify adequate fuel quantity. (2) Set mixture to RICH (in naturally aspirated engines) or appropriate setting for altitude. (3) Use primer if available, priming several strokes to inject fuel into cylinders. (4) Open throttle slightly (approximately 1/8 to 1/4 inch) to prevent stalling once engine fires. (5) Turn magnetos to BOTH. (6) Engage starter. (7) Once engine fires, close throttle to idle, monitor oil pressure and temperature. The primer is essential for cold starts because fuel does not vaporise easily in a cold engine, and the priming injects liquid fuel directly into the cylinders.
exam_tip: Cold start: Set mixture RICH, use primer (several strokes), throttle 1/8–1/4 open, turn magnetos to BOTH, engage starter, monitor gauges.
key_facts: Fuel selector ON, verify fuel quantity
Set mixture to RICH
Prime engine (inject fuel with primer)
Open throttle 1/8 to 1/4 inch
Turn magnetos to BOTH
Engage starter
Monitor oil pressure and temperature
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Engine starting), POH (Pilot's Operating Handbook), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.24.4

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedure for starting an over-primed (flooded) engine
concept: An over-primed engine has excess fuel in the cylinders, preventing spark plugs from firing (the fuel mixture is too rich to ignite). To clear a flooded engine: (1) Set mixture control to IDLE CUT-OFF or full LEAN (depending on aircraft type) to prevent further fuel entering the cylinders. (2) Open throttle fully to allow maximum airflow. (3) Engage starter for several seconds to crank the engine without ignition, allowing excess fuel to be blown out as aerosol spray. (4) Once excess fuel is cleared (exhaust will show less fuel smell), set mixture back to RICH. (5) Engage starter again to start the engine normally. In cold conditions, flooding is common because fuel does not vaporise easily and multiple primer strokes can result in too much fuel.
exam_tip: Flooded engine: Lean/idle cut-off, open throttle fully, crank without ignition to clear excess fuel, then set mixture RICH and restart.
key_facts: Set mixture to IDLE CUT-OFF or full LEAN
Open throttle fully (maximum airflow)
Engage starter several times to clear excess fuel
Watch/smell exhaust to confirm fuel is clearing
Set mixture back to RICH
Engage starter to start engine normally
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Starting procedures), POH (Pilot's Operating Handbook), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.24.4

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedure for starting a hot engine
concept: A hot engine has fuel that vaporises easily and oil at operating temperature, so less priming is needed. To start a hot engine: (1) Set fuel selector to ON and verify adequate fuel. (2) Set mixture to appropriate setting (may be LEAN in hot conditions to prevent over-richness; consult POH). (3) No primer or minimal primer strokes (fuel vaporises readily). (4) Open throttle slightly, but less than a cold start (approximately 1/16 inch) to prevent vapour lock. (5) Turn magnetos to BOTH. (6) Engage starter. If the engine does not fire, suspect vapour lock: use boost pump to push fuel through, lean the mixture further, and wait for fuel line to cool. Once running, monitor all gauges and allow a brief warmup if oil temperature is above normal.
exam_tip: Hot start: Less or no priming needed, set mixture appropriately, throttle only 1/16 inch (minimal), magnetos BOTH, start. If vapour lock suspected, use boost pump and lean mixture.
key_facts: Fuel selector ON, verify fuel quantity
Mixture: consult POH (may be LEAN in hot conditions)
Minimal or no priming (fuel vaporises readily)
Throttle 1/16 inch (less than cold start)
Magnetos to BOTH, engage starter
If no start: suspect vapour lock; use boost pump, lean mixture
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Hot engine starting, vapour lock), POH, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.24.4

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedure if engine fire occurs during start-up
concept: If fire is visible during engine start-up: (1) KEEP CRANKING the starter. This draws the flame back into the engine where combustion continues, often extinguishing the external fire naturally. (2) If fire stops with continued cranking and the engine catches, the fire was likely a backfire that self-cleared. (3) If fire persists after 5–10 seconds of continuous cranking, STOP cranking and immediately shut off fuel by setting mixture to IDLE CUT-OFF (full lean). (4) Stop fuel flow from fuel selector if available. (5) EVACUATE the aircraft immediately; do not attempt to fight the fire. (6) Activate fire extinguisher system if installed. Do not open engine compartment doors or attempt to use water or other extinguishing agents, as these may worsen the situation. The goal is to stop fuel flow and get clear of the aircraft rapidly.
exam_tip: Engine fire on start: KEEP CRANKING first (often self-clears). If fire persists, stop, mixture to IDLE CUT-OFF, stop fuel flow, EVACUATE, call emergency services.
key_facts: Keep cranking engine (flames drawn inside, often self-clears)
Continue for 5–10 seconds
If fire persists: stop cranking
Set mixture to IDLE CUT-OFF (stop fuel)
Stop fuel flow from fuel selector
EVACUATE aircraft immediately
Call emergency services; do not fight fire manually
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Emergency procedures), POH emergency section, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.24.4

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedure for checking oil pressure after engine start
concept: After the engine starts and ignition is confirmed stable: (1) Allow the engine to idle at low RPM to warm up gradually. (2) Monitor the oil pressure gauge continuously for the first few seconds. (3) Oil pressure should rise to the green arc (normal operating range) within a few seconds of cranking; typical range is 40–100 psi depending on aircraft. (4) If oil pressure does not show green arc within 15–30 seconds of start, STOP the engine immediately and investigate. (5) If oil pressure is zero or in the red arc (dangerously low), shut down the engine; do not fly. (6) After confirming normal oil pressure, continue to monitor during the initial warmup phase. (7) Do not advance power or taxi until oil pressure is in the green arc and stable. Oil pressure is critical for engine health; insufficient pressure means inadequate lubrication and rapid engine damage.
exam_tip: After start, monitor oil pressure gauge. Pressure must reach green arc within 15–30 seconds. If zero or red, STOP engine immediately. Do not advance power until pressure is stable in green arc.
key_facts: Allow engine to idle at low RPM
Monitor oil pressure gauge continuously
Pressure should enter green arc within 15–30 seconds
Typical normal range: 40–100 psi (aircraft-specific)
Zero or red pressure: STOP engine immediately
Do not taxi or advance power until pressure stable and green
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Engine monitoring), POH (normal procedures), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.24.4

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedure for stopping the engine
concept: To shut down the engine properly: (1) Reduce power to idle. (2) Allow engine to cool at idle for approximately 1–2 minutes (especially after high-power flight) to prevent thermal shock and ensure all engine areas cool evenly. (3) Check that all electrical loads (radios, lights) are off or being managed. (4) Set mixture to IDLE CUT-OFF or full LEAN (depending on aircraft) to stop fuel flow. The engine will continue running briefly until residual fuel in the cylinders is consumed, then stop. (5) Turn magnetos to OFF. (6) Turn electrical master switch to OFF (turn off all electrical power). (7) Verify all systems are secured (flaps, trim, etc.). (8) Leave flight controls unlocked or secured per POH. Proper shutdown prevents damage from thermal shock, ensures residual heat is managed, and leaves the aircraft in safe condition.
exam_tip: Engine shutdown: Reduce to idle, cool engine 1–2 minutes, set mixture to IDLE CUT-OFF, turn magnetos OFF, master switch OFF. Verify all systems secured.
key_facts: Reduce power to idle
Allow engine to cool at idle 1–2 minutes (thermal shock prevention)
Set mixture to IDLE CUT-OFF
Turn magnetos to OFF
Turn master electrical switch to OFF
Verify all systems secured (flaps, trim, controls)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Engine shutdown), POH (normal procedures), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.24.4

syllabus_desc: Explain the reasons for avoidance of rapid power changes
concept: Rapid throttle openings risk overboosting, detonation, and excessive mechanical stress on the engine. Rapid throttle closure causes sudden cooling of hot engine components (thermal shock), which can lead to cracking of cylinder heads or barrels. Smooth, gradual power changes allow the engine, propeller, and airframe to adjust without excessive stress. Additionally, rapid power increases can cause the propeller to overspeed if the blade angle is fine (low pitch).
exam_tip: Rapid opening risks detonation and overboosting. Rapid closing risks thermal shock (cracking). The answer is always: make power changes smooth and gradual.
key_facts: Rapid opening: risk of overboosting, detonation, mechanical stress
Rapid closing: thermal shock from sudden cooling, risk of cracking
Propeller may overspeed with sudden power increase (fine pitch)
Smooth, gradual changes protect the engine
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2, Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.24.6

syllabus_desc: Explain the necessity for monitoring and cross-checking engine instruments
concept: Engine instruments must be monitored continuously during flight to detect abnormal conditions before they cause damage. Key instruments include: oil pressure (low pressure = serious problem), oil temperature (high temp = overheating), cylinder head temperature (high CHT = detonation risk), RPM (overspeed risk), manifold pressure, fuel pressure, and fuel quantity. Cross-checking means comparing related instruments — for example, high CHT with normal oil temperature suggests a localised problem; high oil temperature with normal CHT may indicate an oil system issue. Any abnormal reading requires investigation and potentially reducing power or diverting.
exam_tip: Monitor instruments continuously. Cross-check related instruments against each other. Any abnormal reading requires action — don't ignore it.
key_facts: Monitor oil pressure, oil temp, CHT, RPM, MAP, fuel continuously
Cross-check related instruments against each other
Low oil pressure = serious — land as soon as practicable
High CHT = detonation risk — reduce power, enrich mixture
Any abnormal reading requires investigation and action
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (instrument monitoring), Module 1.7, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.24.8

syllabus_desc: State the possible causes of rough running or excessive vibration
concept: Rough running causes include: magneto or spark plug failure (check with mag check), carburettor icing (apply carb heat), incorrect mixture setting (too rich or too lean), fouled spark plugs (carbon deposits), fuel contamination (water or debris), partially blocked fuel filter, air leak in the induction system, damaged propeller (vibration), and engine mount looseness. Excessive vibration may also indicate a cracked or damaged propeller blade, loose engine mount bolts, or an accessory drive problem.
exam_tip: Rough running: check carb heat first (most common cause in flight), then mixture, then magnetos. Vibration could be propeller damage — a very different problem requiring immediate attention.
key_facts: Magneto or spark plug failure
Carburettor icing
Incorrect mixture (too rich or too lean)
Fouled spark plugs
Fuel contamination
Damaged propeller (vibration)
Loose engine mounts
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (rough running), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.24.10

syllabus_desc: Describe the actions for identifying and rectifying rough running or vibration
concept: Systematic troubleshooting: apply carburettor heat (to check for carb ice — if RPM recovers, ice was the cause); adjust mixture (lean or enrich to check for mixture problem); check magnetos individually (switch to L then R — if rough on one, that magneto or its plugs may be faulty); check fuel selector (ensure correct tank selected); check fuel pressure (low pressure may indicate pump or filter issue). If vibration rather than rough running, check for visible propeller damage if possible and reduce power to minimise stress. If the problem cannot be resolved, divert to the nearest suitable airfield.
exam_tip: Work through the checklist: carb heat → mixture → mag check → fuel selector → fuel pressure. If in doubt, reduce power and divert.
key_facts: Apply carb heat (check for carb ice)
Adjust mixture (check for rich/lean problem)
Check magnetos individually (identify faulty mag)
Check fuel selector and fuel pressure
If propeller vibration: reduce power, check for damage
If unresolved: divert to nearest suitable airfield
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (troubleshooting), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.24.12

syllabus_desc: State the possible causes of sudden engine failure and in-flight trouble check remedies
concept: Sudden engine failure causes: fuel exhaustion (ran out of fuel), fuel starvation (fuel on board but not reaching engine — wrong tank selected, blocked line, vapour lock), ignition failure (both magnetos failed — very rare), severe carburettor icing, structural engine failure (catastrophic but rare). In-flight trouble check: maintain best glide speed immediately; check fuel selector (switch tanks); check mixture (set to rich); check carb heat (apply full); check magnetos (ensure on BOTH); check primer (ensure locked); attempt engine restart. If restart fails, commit to a forced landing and declare an emergency.
exam_tip: First action: establish best glide speed. Then work through: fuel selector → mixture → carb heat → magnetos → primer → restart attempt. If no restart, commit to forced landing.
key_facts: Fuel exhaustion: no fuel remaining
Fuel starvation: wrong tank, blocked line, vapour lock
Ignition failure (rare)
Severe carburettor icing
Immediate action: best glide speed
Check: fuel selector, mixture, carb heat, magnetos, primer
Attempt restart; if fails, forced landing and declare emergency
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (engine failure), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.24.14

syllabus_desc: Describe the types of systems typically electrically operated in a light aircraft
concept: Electrically operated systems include: the starter motor, radios and communication equipment, navigation instruments and avionics, cockpit and navigation lights, pitot heat, fuel gauges and other engine instruments, autopilot (if fitted), electric flaps and trim (on some aircraft), and stall warning systems. Powered by an alternator (engine-driven) that charges a battery, distributed through a bus bar, and protected by circuit breakers.
exam_tip: Remember the ignition system (magnetos) is NOT dependent on the aircraft electrical system — this is a common trick.
key_facts: Starter motor, radios, navigation instruments, lights
Pitot heat, fuel gauges, engine instruments
Autopilot, electric flaps/trim (if fitted), stall warning
Powered by alternator charging a battery
Distributed via bus bar, protected by circuit breakers
Magneto ignition is NOT dependent on the electrical system
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2, Module 1.7, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.26.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the battery and its function in a light aircraft electrical system
concept: The battery is a rechargeable electrochemical energy source (typically 12V or 24V depending on aircraft) that provides electrical power to start the engine via the starter motor and provides backup power if the alternator fails. Lead-acid batteries are most common in light aircraft. The battery supplies current during the engine start, when all magnetos and electrical systems must operate. Once the engine starts and the alternator (or generator) reaches sufficient RPM, the alternator takes over supplying power to all electrical loads and charging the battery. The battery voltage must be correct for the aircraft system (12V or 24V); installing the wrong voltage battery can damage electrical equipment.
exam_tip: Battery: 12V or 24V, provides starting power and backup. Once engine running, alternator charges battery and supplies power. Correct voltage is critical.
key_facts: Rechargeable electrochemical power source
Typical voltage: 12V or 24V (aircraft-dependent)
Provides starting power to engine
Backup power if alternator fails
Lead-acid construction most common
Must be correct voltage for aircraft system
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (Electrical systems), ATC Aircraft General Knowledge, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.26.4

syllabus_desc: Describe the alternator/generator and its function in a light aircraft electrical system
concept: The alternator (or older-style generator) is an engine-driven electrical machine that generates electricity to power all aircraft electrical systems and charge the battery. The alternator is mechanically coupled to the engine via a belt drive and begins generating electricity once the engine reaches approximately 500–1000 RPM. The alternator supplies power to the bus bar (main electrical distribution point) and to the battery charging circuit. The output of the alternator is regulated by a voltage regulator to maintain a constant voltage (typically 28V DC in 24V systems, 14V DC in 12V systems), preventing overcharging or undercharging of the battery. The alternator is more reliable than older generators and produces power efficiently across the entire RPM range used in flight.
exam_tip: Alternator: engine-driven, generates electricity at ~500–1000 RPM, powers all loads and charges battery. Voltage regulator maintains constant output voltage.
key_facts: Engine-driven electrical generator
Generates electricity once engine reaches ~500–1000 RPM
Powers all aircraft electrical systems
Charges battery during engine operation
Belt-driven from engine crankshaft
Voltage regulator maintains constant output voltage
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (Alternator/generator), ATC Aircraft General Knowledge, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.26.4

syllabus_desc: Describe the bus bar and its function in a light aircraft electrical system
concept: The bus bar is the main electrical distribution point or junction in the aircraft electrical system. All electrical power from the alternator and battery flows to the bus bar, which then distributes power to individual circuits: engine instruments, avionics, lights, radio, fuel pump, landing gear, flaps, and other electrical loads. The bus bar simplifies circuit distribution and allows a single master switch to control all power. In multi-engine aircraft or large aircraft, there may be multiple bus bars (primary, secondary) to segregate systems. The bus bar is typically located in or near the electrical panel and is protected by a main circuit breaker or fuses upstream.
exam_tip: Bus bar: main electrical distribution point. All power flows through bus bar to individual circuits. Simplifies distribution; main breaker upstream protects it.
key_facts: Main electrical distribution junction
Receives power from alternator and battery
Distributes power to all aircraft circuits
All loads connect via bus bar (instruments, lights, radio, fuel pump)
Typically one or more bus bars in larger aircraft
Protected by main breaker or fuses upstream
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (Electrical systems architecture), ATC Aircraft General Knowledge, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.26.4

syllabus_desc: Describe the voltage regulator and voltmeter, and the overvoltage light in a light aircraft electrical system
concept: The voltage regulator is an automatic control device that maintains a constant output voltage from the alternator, typically 28V DC in 24V systems (or 14V DC in 12V systems), regardless of engine RPM or electrical load. The voltage regulator protects against overcharging the battery (which causes damage and reduces battery life) and ensures consistent voltage to all electrical equipment. The voltmeter is a cockpit instrument that displays the alternator output voltage (or battery voltage if alternator is offline); normal indication is in the green arc. The overvoltage light (OV light) is a warning light that illuminates if the alternator output voltage exceeds a safe limit (typically >30V in 24V systems), indicating voltage regulator failure. If the OV light illuminates in flight, the pilot should reduce electrical load (turn off non-essential systems) and land as soon as practicable to investigate.
exam_tip: Voltage regulator: maintains constant alternator output (~28V in 24V systems). Voltmeter displays system voltage. OV light warns if voltage exceeds limits (regulator failure).
key_facts: Voltage regulator: maintains constant alternator output voltage
Typical output: 28V DC (24V system) or 14V DC (12V system)
Protects against overcharging and voltage surges
Voltmeter: cockpit instrument showing system voltage
Normal range: green arc on voltmeter
OV light: illuminates if voltage exceeds safe limit (regulator failure warning)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (Electrical system components), POH, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.26.4

syllabus_desc: Describe the ammeter and its function in a light aircraft electrical system
concept: The ammeter is a cockpit instrument that measures the flow of electrical current (in amperes) in the electrical system. A positive ammeter indication (charging current) means the alternator is supplying more current than the aircraft loads are consuming, and the excess current is charging the battery. A zero or near-zero indication means the alternator is supplying exactly the electrical load demand. A negative ammeter indication (discharge current) means the battery is supplying current to the aircraft (alternator is not generating sufficient power, possibly due to low RPM or alternator failure). During normal flight at cruise power, the ammeter typically shows a small positive charge rate, indicating the alternator is charging the battery while powering the aircraft. If the ammeter shows negative (discharge) during normal cruise, the alternator may have failed, and the pilot must land to investigate. The ammeter is a critical instrument for monitoring electrical system health.
exam_tip: Ammeter: shows charging (+) or discharge (−) current in amperes. Positive = alternator charging battery. Negative = battery discharging (alternator may have failed).
key_facts: Measures electrical current flow (amperes)
Positive indication: charging current (alternator > loads)
Zero indication: alternator supplying loads exactly
Negative indication: battery discharging (load > alternator output)
Normal cruise: small positive charge rate
Negative discharge during cruise: indicates alternator failure
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (Electrical monitoring), POH, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.26.4

syllabus_desc: Describe the master switch and battery/alternator switches in a light aircraft electrical system
concept: The master switch is the main electrical power control that connects (or disconnects) the battery to the entire aircraft electrical system. Turning the master switch ON energises the bus bar and all electrical circuits. Turning it OFF de-energises the system, stopping all electrical power except to systems connected directly to the battery for emergency use (e.g., emergency beacon in some aircraft). In many light aircraft, the master switch has two positions or a split design: a main position (ON/OFF) controls all power, and a battery-only position allows the battery to power essential systems (lights, radio) without the alternator running. The battery/alternator switch (or ALT position) allows the pilot to isolate the alternator while keeping the battery powered if the alternator is malfunctioning. If the alternator fails during flight, the pilot can switch to battery-only, ensuring continued electrical power to essential systems until landing.
exam_tip: Master switch: main ON/OFF control for all electrical power. Battery/alternator switch allows isolating alternator if failed. Battery-only position provides emergency power.
key_facts: Master switch controls main electrical power
ON: energises entire electrical system
OFF: de-energises all circuits (except emergency systems)
Split design: main and battery-only positions
Battery/alternator switch: isolates alternator if failed
Battery-only allows continued power for essential systems
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (Master switch control), POH, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.26.4

syllabus_desc: Describe fuses and circuit breakers in a light aircraft electrical system
concept: Fuses and circuit breakers protect individual electrical circuits from overcurrent (too much current flowing due to a short circuit or overload). A fuse is a one-use device containing a metal filament that melts if current exceeds its rated value, breaking the circuit. A circuit breaker is a reusable switch that trips (opens) if current exceeds its rated value and can be reset by the pilot. Modern aircraft typically use circuit breakers rather than fuses because they are reusable and allow in-flight troubleshooting (pilot can attempt to reset a tripped breaker). Each major circuit (engine, avionics, lights, fuel pump, landing gear, etc.) has its own fuse or breaker with a specific amperage rating. If a breaker pops (trips) during flight, it indicates an electrical fault in that circuit; the pilot should not repeatedly reset the same breaker as this may allow dangerous current flow or cause fire. If a fuse blows, it must be replaced with one of the correct amperage rating (never substitute a higher-rated fuse).
exam_tip: Fuses and circuit breakers protect circuits from overcurrent. Fuses are one-time, breakers are reusable and trippable. Each circuit has its own rated protection device.
key_facts: Protect individual circuits from overcurrent
Fuses: one-time use, filament melts if overloaded
Circuit breakers: reusable switches, trip if overloaded
Each major circuit has its own rated device
Pilot can reset tripped breaker (if safe)
Never substitute higher-rated fuse; use correct amperage rating
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (Circuit protection), POH, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.26.4

syllabus_desc: Describe why high power should be avoided on the ground before engine start
concept: High engine power on the ground before engine start should be avoided to prevent damage to the aircraft and electrical system. Running the engine at high RPM while stationary causes uncontrolled acceleration of the propeller, which can damage the airframe, raise dangerous noise levels in the cockpit, and cause strong prop wash that may damage nearby aircraft or equipment. Additionally, high-power ground operation does not allow the engine to warm up evenly, stresses the engine components prematurely, and increases fuel consumption and spark plug fouling. The engine should be started at idle and allowed to warm at low RPM before power is increased for taxi or flight.
exam_tip: No high power on ground before start. Prevents damage to airframe, engine, and nearby equipment. Let engine warm at idle before advancing throttle.
key_facts: High power on ground causes uncontrolled prop acceleration
Risk of airframe vibration damage
Strong prop wash damages nearby aircraft and equipment
High-power operation on cold engine causes premature wear
Increases spark plug fouling in cold conditions
Start at idle, allow warmup before advancing power
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Engine ground operation), POH, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.26.6

syllabus_desc: Describe why starting with radios off is a good practice
concept: Starting the engine with radios (and other electrical systems) off conserves electrical power and prevents electrical spikes or interference caused by starter motor operation. The starter motor draws very large current from the battery (several hundred amperes), which causes a significant voltage drop in the electrical system. If radios are operating during this voltage dip, it can cause interference, momentary loss of radio function, or potential damage to sensitive avionics. By switching radios off before starting, the battery power is reserved for the starter motor and engine starting systems. Once the engine is running and the alternator has stabilised its output (at cruise RPM), radios can be turned back on. This practice also reduces overall electrical load during the startup transient period.
exam_tip: Start with radios off: starter motor draws huge current, causing voltage dips. This prevents radio interference and damage to avionics during startup.
key_facts: Starter motor draws very large current (several hundred amperes)
Large current causes significant voltage drop in system
Radio operation during voltage dip causes interference
Can damage sensitive avionics during startup transient
Turn radios on after engine stable at cruise RPM
Reserves battery power for starter and essential starting systems
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Starting procedures), POH, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.26.6

syllabus_desc: Describe why prolonged starter motor operation should be avoided
concept: The starter motor is designed for short bursts (10–15 seconds per attempt) of high current draw. Prolonged starter operation causes the motor to overheat, potentially burning out the windings and rendering the starter inoperable. Additionally, prolonged cranking may flood the engine if the engine does not fire (fuel continues to be drawn into cylinders without combustion). If the engine does not start within 15 seconds, the pilot should stop cranking, pause for 30 seconds to allow the starter to cool, and attempt again. Excessive starter use can also flatten the battery quickly, leaving insufficient electrical power for subsequent restart attempts.
exam_tip: Limit starter motor to 10–15 seconds per attempt. Prolonged use causes overheating, flooding, and battery depletion. Stop, cool, and retry if needed.
key_facts: Starter motor rated for short bursts only (~10–15 seconds)
Prolonged operation causes overheating and burnout
Extended cranking floods engine (unburned fuel accumulates)
Excessive use depletes battery charge
If no start: stop, wait 30 seconds to cool, retry
Never attempt continuous cranking for >30 seconds
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Starting procedures), POH, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.26.6

syllabus_desc: Describe why the starter must be released when the engine is running
concept: Once the engine starts and begins firing, the starter motor must be released (ignition switch returned to normal RUN position or starter switch released) immediately. If the starter remains engaged while the engine is running, the engine can backfire and drive the starter motor backward (causing the pinion gear to grind against the starter ring gear on the flywheel). This rapidly damages both the starter motor and the engine's flywheel ring gear. Backdriving the starter can also create dangerous vibration and mechanical damage. Modern starters have a one-way overrunning clutch that prevents the starter motor from being driven backward by the engine, but the clutch can be damaged by extended contact after engine start. Good practice is to release the starter immediately when engine ignition is confirmed (engine catches and begins self-sustaining combustion).
exam_tip: Release starter immediately when engine fires. Prolonged engagement after start causes backdriving, grinding, and damage to starter and ring gear.
key_facts: Release starter immediately when engine catches
If starter remains engaged, engine can backfire
Backfire drives starter backward (backdriving)
Causes grinding of pinion gear on ring gear
Rapidly damages starter motor and flywheel ring gear
Can create dangerous vibration and mechanical damage
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Starting procedures), POH, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.26.6

syllabus_desc: Describe why electrical system checks should be performed after engine start and monitored in flight
concept: After engine start, the pilot must verify that the electrical system is functioning correctly by checking: (1) Ammeter reading (should show positive charging current), (2) Voltmeter reading (should be in green arc, typically 24–28V for 24V system or 12–15V for 12V system), (3) No illumination of alternator warning light or OV light (if installed), and (4) All essential instruments have stable power. During flight, the ammeter and voltmeter must be monitored continuously. A negative ammeter reading during cruise (battery discharging) indicates alternator failure; in this case, the pilot should reduce electrical load and divert to nearest aerodrome. A voltmeter reading outside the green arc or an illuminated OV light also indicates a problem. Continuous monitoring of electrical instruments ensures early detection of system failure before critical power is lost.
exam_tip: After start: check ammeter (positive charge), voltmeter (green arc), warning lights off, instruments stable. Monitor continuously in flight for early failure detection.
key_facts: Check ammeter after start: should show positive charging
Check voltmeter: should be in green arc (24–28V or 12–15V)
Verify no alternator warning light or OV light illuminated
Confirm all essential instruments have stable power
Monitor ammeter and voltmeter continuously in flight
Negative ammeter in cruise = alternator failure; reduce load, divert
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (In-flight monitoring), POH, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.26.6

syllabus_desc: Describe why ancillary systems should be switched off before engine shut-down
concept: Before engine shut-down, non-essential ancillary systems (radio, avionics, lights) should be switched off to prevent the battery from supplying unnecessary current to these systems while the alternator is no longer generating power. As engine RPM decays during shut-down, the alternator output decreases and eventually stops when RPM falls below the minimum generation threshold (~500–1000 RPM). If ancillary systems remain on during this transition, the battery must supply all power, causing a rapid discharge. Switching these systems off before reducing engine RPM to idle ensures the battery is not unnecessarily drained, preserving reserve charge for the final stages of shut-down (eg magneto and master switch operations). This practice extends battery life and ensures sufficient charge remains for future engine starts.
exam_tip: Before shutdown: switch off radios, lights, and ancillary systems. Prevents battery drain as alternator output decreases during engine deceleration.
key_facts: Switch off radio before reducing RPM
Switch off lights and non-essential systems
Alternator output decreases as engine RPM decreases
Generator stops when RPM falls below ~500–1000
Battery must supply all power after generator stops
Prevents unnecessary battery discharge during shutdown
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Engine shutdown), POH, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.26.6

syllabus_desc: Describe why the battery master switch should be turned off before leaving the aircraft
concept: The battery master switch should be turned off before leaving the aircraft to prevent the battery from powering any electrical systems while the aircraft is unattended. Even with the engine off and ignition switch off, some electrical circuits may have residual power or parasitic drain (clock, avionics with backup batteries, beacon lights). Leaving the master switch on allows the battery to continue discharging, slowly draining it over hours or days. A fully discharged battery may prevent engine start on the next flight and can suffer permanent damage (lead-acid batteries left in a discharged state develop lead sulphate crystal buildup that reduces future capacity). Turning off the master switch ensures the aircraft electrical system is completely de-energised and the battery is protected from parasitic drain. This is standard procedure in all aircraft.
exam_tip: Before leaving aircraft: master switch OFF. Prevents parasitic drain and battery depletion. Protects battery from damage due to discharged state.
key_facts: Battery master switch OFF before exiting aircraft
Prevents parasitic drain (clocks, beacon lights, avionics)
Ensures complete de-energisation of electrical system
Battery discharge over time damages lead-acid batteries
Discharged battery may not start engine next flight
Protects battery from lead sulphate crystal buildup
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Post-flight procedures), POH, AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.26.6

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms of excessive charge rate in the electrical system
concept: Excessive charge rate occurs when the voltage regulator fails and allows the alternator to output voltage higher than the normal regulated value (typically >30V in a 24V system or >15V in a 12V system). Symptoms include: (1) Voltmeter reading in the red arc (above normal), (2) Ammeter showing abnormally high positive (charging) current, (3) Overvoltage warning light (OV light) illuminated if installed, (4) Possible smell of burning or overheating coming from the electrical system or battery, (5) Battery may appear to be boiling or steaming (in lead-acid batteries with removable caps). Excessive voltage damages electrical components, boils battery electrolyte, and can cause component failure or even fire.
