Avoid extreme manoeuvres when relying on gyro instruments
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (gyro toppling), AC 61-3 Rev 31 §12.40.16

syllabus_desc: Describe the basic principles and operation of a GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System).
concept: GNSS uses signals from orbiting satellites to determine precise position, velocity and time. At least 24 satellites are distributed in six orbital planes at 20,200 km altitude. The receiver calculates position by measuring signal travel time from multiple satellites (trilateration). GPS, GLONASS, Galileo and BeiDou are GNSS systems. Accuracy typically 5-10m in civilian mode.
exam_tip: GNSS needs minimum 4 satellites for 3D position fix (3 for 2D).
key_facts: Constellation of 24+ satellites
Trilateration method
Atomic clocks on satellites
Ground augmentation systems
Accuracy 5-10m civilian
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.42.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the limitations and failure indications of a GNSS system.
concept: GNSS limitations include: signal blockage in tunnels/under trees, multipath errors from signal reflection, atmospheric ionospheric delays, poor satellite geometry (high dilution of precision/DOP), and intentional signal degradation. Failure indications: loss of signal, jumping position fixes, high DOP values, no GPS fix when previously locked, and integrity warnings. Cold start initialization takes 30+ seconds; warm start 15 seconds.
exam_tip: High DOP indicates poor satellite geometry; avoid flying under poor DOP conditions.
key_facts: Signal blockage/multipath errors
Ionospheric delays
Dilution of Precision (DOP)
Cold/warm/hot start times
Loss of signal indication
Receiver integrity warning
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.42.4

syllabus_desc: Briefly describe the basic function and operation of TCAS.
concept: Traffic Collision Avoidance System uses aircraft transponders to detect nearby aircraft and predict collision. TCAS I provides Traffic Advisories (TA) to increase crew awareness. TCAS II adds Resolution Advisories (RA) commanding vertical maneuvers to avoid collision. Range up to 40 nautical miles. System uses distance, altitude and closure rate to determine threat level. Operates independently of ground control.
exam_tip: TCAS II provides climb/descend commands (RA); TCAS I only warns.
key_facts: Two TCAS levels (I and II)
TA and RA advisories
40nm detection range
Transponder required
Closure rate calculation
Vertical avoidance only
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.44.2

syllabus_desc: Briefly describe the basic function and operation of TAWS (Terrain Awareness and Warning System).
concept: TAWS prevents controlled flight into terrain (CFIT) using terrain database, GPS altitude, barometric altitude, aircraft attitude and radio altimeter. System displays terrain on cockpit display and provides aural warnings when aircraft approaches high terrain or descends below safe altitude for location. Modes include enveloping, display and combined modes. Forward-looking capability predicts terrain hazard.
exam_tip: TAWS warns of terrain ahead; don't ignore aural warnings.
key_facts: Terrain database stored on-board
GPS and barometric altitude used
Forward-looking prediction
Aural warnings and alerts
Display in cockpit
Can be terrain-referenced approach
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.46.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and operation of a typical EFIS cockpit display system.
concept: Electronic Flight Instrument System replaces mechanical instruments with integrated digital displays. Primary Flight Display (PFD) shows attitude, airspeed, altitude, heading and vertical speed. Navigation Display (ND) shows flight plan, position, navigation sources. EFIS integrates data from multiple sensors and provides redundancy. Displays use LCD or CRT technology. System can be configured for single or dual pilot operation.
exam_tip: EFIS shows PFD and ND; cross-check with standby instruments.
key_facts: Primary Flight Display (PFD)
Navigation Display (ND)
Integrated data sources
Sensor fusion
Built-in redundancy
Configuration flexibility
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.48.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the air data computer.
concept: The Air Data Computer (ADC) receives pitot and static pressure inputs from the pitot-static probe and processes these to calculate and output airspeed (IAS/CAS/TAS), altitude (QFE/QNH), vertical speed (rate of climb/descent), outside air temperature (OAT), and Mach number. The ADC is the primary source of air data information for the EFIS display system.
exam_tip: ADC converts static and pitot pressure inputs to airspeed, altitude, and vertical speed. Understand the difference between IAS, CAS, and TAS.
key_facts: Receives pitot and static pressure inputs
Calculates airspeed (IAS, CAS, TAS)
Calculates altitude and vertical speed
Outputs outside air temperature (OAT)
Calculates Mach number
Central source of air data for EFIS
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.48.4(a)

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the signal generator.
concept: The Signal Generator (SG) receives magnetic heading input from the aircraft's compass system and converts this analog compass heading signal into a digital output signal for display on the EFIS flight display. The SG acts as an interface between the compass and the digital EFIS system.
exam_tip: Signal Generator converts compass heading to EFIS display format. Don't confuse with other heading sources (inertial, GPS).
key_facts: Receives analog magnetic heading input
Converts compass signal to digital format
Outputs heading to EFIS display
Interface between compass and display system
Accuracy depends on compass accuracy
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.48.4(b)

syllabus_desc: Describe the input data sources to a typical basic EFIS flight display system.
concept: A typical basic EFIS flight display system integrates multiple data sources: pitot-static probe (airspeed and altitude via ADC), magnetic compass (heading via Signal Generator), inertial reference system (attitude and rate information), GPS receiver (position and ground track), radio altimeter (height above ground), engine sensors (power and performance parameters), and sometimes angle-of-attack sensor. This multi-source integration provides redundancy and cross-checking of critical flight data.
exam_tip: Know the main input sources: pitot-static, compass, inertial, GPS, radio altimeter, and engine sensors. Understand redundancy.
key_facts: Pitot-static probe for airspeed and altitude
Magnetic compass for heading input
Inertial reference for attitude and rates
GPS receiver for position and track
Radio altimeter for height above ground
Engine sensors for power parameters
Angle-of-attack sensor (some systems)
Data integration and cross-checking
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.48.4(c)

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and operation of a typical basic AHRS (Attitude Heading Reference System).
concept: AHRS is an inertial reference system that continuously calculates aircraft pitch, roll and heading using accelerometers and gyroscopes. Uses triple-redundant solid-state gyros and accelerometers. Integrates with air data to provide attitude without reliance on magnetic compass. Provides pitch/roll accurate to ±2-5 degrees. Self-aligns on ground during initialization (typically 2-5 minutes). Outputs attitude data to flight displays and autopilot.
exam_tip: AHRS provides independent attitude; compares with magnetic compass for heading.
key_facts: Solid-state gyroscopes and accelerometers
Pitch and roll calculation
Heading reference integration
Ground alignment requirement
Continuous operation
±2-5° accuracy typical
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.48.6

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and operation of an aircraft ELT.
concept: Emergency Locator Transmitter (ELT) transmits distress signals on 121.5 MHz and 406 MHz in event of aircraft accident or manual activation. Automatic ELTs (ELT-AF) activate on impact via G-switch detecting deceleration >5g. Manual ELTs (ELT-M) require crew activation. 406 MHz signals contain aircraft registration and location data enabling satellite detection (COSPAS-SARSAT system). Battery provides minimum 48 hours transmission. Antenna must be protected from damage to function properly.
exam_tip: ELT transmits on 406 MHz for satellite detection; 121.5 MHz for direction finding.
key_facts: Dual frequency (121.5 & 406 MHz)
Automatic G-switch activation
Manual activation capability
48-hour battery life
Registration data in 406 signal
Satellite detection system
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.50.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the principle components of aircraft engine air and liquid cooling systems.
concept: Air-cooled engines: fins on cylinder heads/barrels dissipate heat directly to airflow; controlled via cowl flaps. Liquid-cooled systems: coolant circulates through engine jacket cooling passages, flows to radiator where airflow removes heat, then returns to engine. Components include: coolant pump (driven by engine), thermostat (maintains optimal temperature), radiator (heat exchanger), overflow tank, coolant hoses. Liquid systems more efficient but heavier.
exam_tip: Cowl flaps control air cooling; thermostat regulates liquid cooling temperature.
key_facts: Finned cylinders air cooling
Liquid coolant circulation
Thermostat temperature control
Radiator heat exchange
Coolant pump operation
Overflow tank capacity
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.52.2

syllabus_desc: Describe engine overheating and its causes.
concept: Engine overheating occurs when cooling capacity cannot dissipate the heat generated during combustion. Causes include: high power settings, high ambient temperature, high humidity reducing air cooling efficiency, blockage of cooling airflow (debris, ice), thermostat failure (stuck closed), coolant pump failure, low coolant level, or high engine load (climb/high altitude cruise). Overheating causes cylinder head temperature to rise above maximum allowable limits, leading to detonation, piston/ring damage, and potential engine failure.
exam_tip: Overheating → detonation → engine damage. Check cooling system function and ambient conditions before high-power climbs.
key_facts: Caused by excess heat and insufficient cooling
High power settings increase heat generation
High ambient temperature reduces cooling
Blockage of cooling airflow causes overheating
Thermostat stuck closed prevents cooling
Low coolant level reduces cooling capacity
Overheating leads to detonation
Cylinder head temperature limits are critical
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.52.4(a)

syllabus_desc: Describe engine overcooling and its causes.
concept: Engine overcooling occurs when cooling capacity is excessive, allowing engine temperature to drop below the operating range. Causes include: high airspeed in descent (excessive cooling air), thermostat failure (stuck open), over-lenient cooling restrictions, or cold ambient conditions. Overcooling causes poor fuel atomization, incomplete combustion, increased engine wear, and inefficient operation. Cold engines produce more residual gases and higher friction.
exam_tip: Overcooling → poor combustion → rough running. Use cowl flaps and monitor CHT carefully in descent.
key_facts: Excessive cooling causes low cylinder head temperature
High airspeed in descent increases cooling
Thermostat stuck open prevents temperature rise
Cold ambient temperature contributes
Poor fuel vaporization in cold engine
Incomplete combustion and rough running
Increased engine wear and friction
Inefficient engine operation
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.52.4(b)

syllabus_desc: Explain the correct handling of engine cowl flaps when fitted.
concept: Cowl flaps are variable openings on engine cowling controlling airflow and cooling. Open cowl flaps fully for ground operations, takeoff, and climb (maximum cooling needed at high power). Close cowl flaps during cruise to reduce drag and improve speed/endurance. Monitor engine temperature continuously when adjusting flaps. During descent avoid complete closure initially as descent already increases cooling. Reopen flaps if temperature approaches limits. Most aircraft have cowl flap position indicator or temperature gauge to guide operation.
exam_tip: Open flaps for climb, close for cruise; always monitor engine temp.
key_facts: Full open for takeoff/climb
Closed for cruise efficiency
Temperature monitoring during changes
Prevents overheating/overcooling
Drag reduction when closed
Position indicator reading
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.52.6

syllabus_desc: Describe the two common types of undercarriage system (tricycle/tail wheel).
concept: Tricycle gear: nose wheel forward, two main wheels aft provides better ground handling and is easier to land. Nose wheel steerable, provides directional control on ground. Tail wheel gear: two main wheels forward, tail wheel aft provides better view during takeoff. Main wheels typically fixed (non-steerable), tail wheel swivels. Tail wheel prone to ground loop in strong crosswinds. Tricycle more stable and forgiving; tail wheel requires more skill.
exam_tip: Tricycle gear: easier to land and stable. Tail wheel: prone to ground loop.
key_facts: Tricycle: nose wheel forward, two mains aft
Tail wheel: two mains forward, tail aft
Nose wheel steering capability
Tail wheel swivel action
Ground loop risk (tail wheel)
Takeoff view differences
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.54.2

syllabus_desc: Explain typical steering and braking systems.
concept: Steering: nose wheel steerable via control column or rudder pedal input (mechanical or hydraulic linkage). Tail wheel aircraft use rudder pedals connected to tail wheel via cable/mechanical linkage for steering. Braking: hydraulic disc brakes on main landing gear wheels, operated via brake pedals. Left pedal controls left brake, right pedal controls right brake. Independent braking allows differential braking for directional control. Brake fluid pressure gauges monitor system. Parking brake holds aircraft stationary.
exam_tip: Differential braking provides directional control on ground.
key_facts: Nose wheel mechanical/hydraulic steering
Rudder pedal steering in tail wheel
Hydraulic disc brakes
Independent left/right control
Brake pressure indication
Parking brake system
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.54.4

syllabus_desc: Explain the precautions for the use of each type.
concept: Tricycle precautions: avoid nose wheel shimmy through smooth braking and steering, don't land excessively nose-down (prop strike risk), ensure adequate taxi speed for steering effectiveness. Tail wheel precautions: maintain directional control to prevent ground loop, use forward slip if needed to increase visibility during landing, avoid sharp turns at speed, taxi slowly in crosswind to prevent sudden swerve, use differential braking cautiously to avoid inducing ground loop.
exam_tip: Tricycle: watch prop strike. Tail wheel: prevent ground loop.
key_facts: Tricycle nose wheel shimmy risks
Nose-down landing hazard
Tail wheel directional control
Ground loop prevention
Crosswind taxi techniques
Speed control importance
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.54.6

syllabus_desc: Show how CL varies with use of flaps and control surfaces.
concept: Coefficient of Lift (CL) increases with flap extension through increased camber and effective wing area. With flaps fully retracted CL increases with angle of attack until stall angle. Extension of flaps increases maximum CL (stall limit increases), shifts lift curve leftward (lower stall airspeed), but increases drag significantly. Flap extension of 10-15 degrees increases CL gradually; beyond 40 degrees additional CL gain minimal while drag increases dramatically. Elevator deflection changes pitch attitude, changing angle of attack and thus CL. Ailerons primarily control roll but slight changes in CL occur due to induced drag differences.
exam_tip: Flaps increase CL and lower stall speed; more flaps = higher drag.
key_facts: Flap extension increases CL
Stall speed decreases with flaps
Drag increases with flaps
Maximum CL limitation
Elevator changes angle of attack
Aileron roll coupling effects
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.56.2

syllabus_desc: Define the aircraft axes of rotation, pitch, roll and yaw.
concept: Three aircraft axes intersect at center of gravity: Pitch axis (lateral axis) runs wing-tip to wing-tip, pitching motion around this axis moves nose up/down. Roll axis (longitudinal axis) runs nose to tail, rolling motion around this axis banks the wings. Yaw axis (vertical axis) points straight down through fuselage, yawing motion around this axis turns the nose left/right.
exam_tip: Pitch: nose up/down. Roll: wing up/down. Yaw: nose left/right.
key_facts: Pitch axis (lateral, wing-tip to wing-tip)
Roll axis (longitudinal, nose to tail)
Yaw axis (vertical, pointing down)
Center of gravity intersection point
Three-axis rotation system
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.58.2

syllabus_desc: Name the flying controls used to affect movement about each axis.
concept: Pitch control: elevator (on horizontal stabilizer tail surface) deflected by control column forward/back. Roll control: ailerons (on trailing edge of wings) deflected oppositely to bank the aircraft. Yaw control: rudder (on vertical stabilizer tail surface) deflected by rudder pedals to turn nose. Elevator controls pitch; ailerons control roll; rudder controls yaw. These are primary flight controls. Trim controls (elevator, aileron, rudder tabs) reduce control forces needed.
exam_tip: Elevator pitch, ailerons roll, rudder yaw.
key_facts: Elevator for pitch (horizontal stabilizer)
Ailerons for roll (wing trailing edge)
Rudder for yaw (vertical stabilizer)
Control column and pedal inputs
Trim tab operation
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.58.4

topic: Attitude Indicator (AI) and Flight Display References
syllabus_desc: Describe pitch attitude as displayed on the attitude indicator.
concept: Pitch attitude is the angle between the aircraft's longitudinal axis and the horizon. The attitude indicator displays pitch attitude as the position of the artificial horizon line relative to the aircraft symbol. Pitch up (nose above horizon) is displayed above the horizon line; pitch down (nose below horizon) is displayed below. The display is scaled in degrees (typically 0°, 5°, 10°, 15°, 20°, 30°). The attitude indicator directly shows the aircraft's pitch without relying on airspeed or altitude inputs.
exam_tip: Pitch attitude = angle of nose relative to horizon. On AI, horizon line shows pitch. Pitch and airspeed interact in performance.
key_facts: Pitch attitude is nose angle relative to horizon
Positive pitch = nose above horizon
Negative pitch = nose below horizon
AI displays pitch scale in degrees
Independent of airspeed or altitude
Critical for aircraft control
Used with bank angle for 3-D aircraft orientation
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.58.6(a)

topic: Attitude Indicator (AI) and Flight Display References
syllabus_desc: Describe bank angle as displayed on the attitude indicator.
concept: Bank angle is the angle between the aircraft's lateral (wing) axis and the horizon. The attitude indicator displays bank angle using a roll pointer that rotates around the instrument face and aligns with degree markings (typically 0°, 5°, 10°, 15°, 20°, 30°, 45°, 60°). A bank angle of 0° indicates wings level (pointer at top). A bank angle of 20° right has the pointer 20° right of top. Bank angle is independent of pitch attitude and is the primary reference for turns.
exam_tip: Bank angle = wing tilt relative to horizon. Roll pointer shows degrees of bank. Essential for instrument flying and turns.
key_facts: Bank angle is wing tilt relative to horizon
Displayed using roll pointer on AI
Degree scale shows bank angle increment
0° = wings level (pointer at top)
Positive bank = right roll pointer deflection
Negative bank = left roll pointer deflection
Independent of pitch attitude
Critical reference for turns and level flight
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.58.6(b)

topic: Attitude Indicator (AI) and Flight Display References
syllabus_desc: Describe yaw as displayed on attitude indicators and heading references.
concept: Yaw is rotation about the aircraft's vertical (Z) axis. The attitude indicator does not directly display yaw, but yaw is indicated by the heading indicator/compass card at the top of the attitude indicator display. Yaw rate is the rate of change of heading in degrees per second. The heading reference shows the magnetic direction the aircraft is pointing (0°–360°) and changes as the aircraft yaws left or right. Yaw is controlled by rudder input and is separate from banking.
exam_tip: Yaw = rotation about vertical axis. Heading indicator shows direction aircraft points. Yaw ≠ bank. Rudder controls yaw.
key_facts: Yaw is rotation about vertical Z axis
Displayed on heading indicator (compass rose)
Shows magnetic direction aircraft points (0–360°)
Yaw rate = rate of heading change
Controlled by rudder input
Independent of pitch and bank
Critical for coordinated flight
Separate from bank/turn indicator
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.58.6(c)

syllabus_desc: Explain the cross-coupling (further) effects of control in roll and yaw.
concept: Cross-coupling effects arise from simultaneous motion about multiple axes. In a roll, the outer wing has higher relative velocity increasing its lift; this causes a yawing moment opposite to roll direction (adverse yaw). Rudder application creates a yawing moment which, if uncoordinated, causes secondary roll due to cross-coupling. Banking in a climb causes pitch change due to changing vertical component of lift. Rolling while rolling causes complex motion; pure roll rarely occurs without coordinated rudder. Dutch roll occurs if rolling oscillation couples with yawing oscillation.
exam_tip: Adverse yaw opposes roll; use rudder to coordinate turn.
key_facts: Adverse yaw from aileron roll
Secondary roll from rudder application
Pitch change in banked climbs
Dutch roll oscillation
Cross-axis coupling effects
Coordination requirement
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.58.8

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of airspeed and power settings on control effectiveness and aircraft attitude.
concept: Control effectiveness increases with airspeed because aerodynamic forces depend on dynamic pressure (0.5 x rho x V squared). At low airspeed, control deflections produce minimal response; at high airspeed, same control input produces large response. Power setting affects attitude through thrust line location (usually above center of gravity creating pitch-up moment). Increasing power tends to pitch nose up; reducing power pitches nose down. High power at low speed demands high angle of attack, low power allows lower angle of attack. Airspeed changes require attitude adjustment to maintain level flight.
exam_tip: Low speed = weak controls; high speed = strong controls. Power pitches nose.
key_facts: Dynamic pressure effect on control
Airspeed-control authority relationship
Thrust line pitch effect
Power setting attitude change
Speed stability coupling
Angle of attack adjustment
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.58.10

syllabus_desc: Explain the function of a basic trim control.
concept: Trim controls (tabs on elevator, aileron, rudder) adjust control surfaces to zero deflection position at desired airspeed, reducing pilot control force requirement. Elevator trim adjusts to achieve hands-off pitch attitude; excessive trim change from neutral warns of CG shift or configuration change. Aileron trim corrects for fuel imbalance or wing damage. Rudder trim reduces rudder pedal force for coordinated flight. Trim must be readjusted when airspeed or configuration (flaps, gear) changes. Excessive trim deflection from neutral indicates problem requiring investigation.
exam_tip: Trim reduces control forces; large changes indicate problem.
key_facts: Trim tab operation principle
Elevator trim pitch adjustment
Aileron trim for balance
Rudder trim for coordination
Airspeed trim adjustment
Configuration trim changes
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.58.12

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirement for the balancing of controls.
concept: Controls require aerodynamic and mechanical balancing to prevent flutter and reduce control forces. Flutter occurs if aerodynamic forces exceed structural stiffness, causing oscillation and potential structural failure. Balance involves: (1) moving control surface hinge axis forward of center of pressure (horn balance); (2) adding balance weight forward of hinge axis (weight balance); (3) providing aerodynamic relief through horn or balance tab. Without balance, large control forces required, flutter risk high, control response sluggish. Balance maintained through design; pilot must use trim to reduce residual forces.
exam_tip: Balance prevents flutter and reduces control forces.
key_facts: Aerodynamic balance necessity
Flutter prevention
Hinge axis positioning
Balance weight application
Balance tab function
Control force reduction
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.58.14

syllabus_desc: Explain the correct method for the use of trim controls.
concept: Establish desired airspeed and configuration first, then adjust trim to achieve hands-off pitch attitude. For elevator trim: pitch to desired attitude using control column, hold attitude, smoothly adjust trim until control pressure zero. For aileron trim: establish desired bank angle, adjust trim until no sideways pressure. For rudder trim: coordinate turn using rudder, adjust trim until feet neutral. Never chase speed with trim; trim is for force removal, not speed control. Always verify trim is within normal range; excessive deflection from neutral indicates problem. Reset trim before significant configuration change (flaps, gear, speed).
exam_tip: Establish attitude first, then trim for zero force.
key_facts: Establish airspeed before trim
Smooth trim adjustment
Hands-off control requirement
Neutral force confirmation
Normal trim range check
Pre-configuration reset
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.58.16

syllabus_desc: State the methods used to obtain aerodynamic balance.
concept: Aerodynamic balance achieved through: (1) Horn balance: part of control surface protrudes ahead of hinge axis providing aerodynamic relief (reduces control deflection needed); (2) Servobalance or overhang: percentage of control surface ahead of hinge axis; (3) Balance tab: auxiliary tab on control surface that moves opposite to control, reducing force required; (4) Antibalance tab: moves with control surface to intentionally increase force (on all-moving surfaces). Most practical aircraft use combination: horn balance for basic relief plus balance tab for fine-tuning. Design ensures control force within pilot capability across speed range.
exam_tip: Horn balance projects ahead of hinge axis for aerodynamic relief.
key_facts: Horn balance concept
Servobalance percentage
Balance tab auxiliary control
Antibalance tab function
Force reduction purpose
Combined balance methods
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.58.18

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirement for using anti-balance tabs on an all-moving tailplane.
concept: All-moving (flying) tailplane rotates entirely about hinge axis with no fixed surface. This design is aerodynamically efficient but creates large hinge moment changes. Without anti-balance tab, control force would be excessively light at high speed (flutter risk) and heavy at low speed. Anti-balance tab moves WITH the tailplane (not opposing it), providing additional moment that increases control force, maintaining proper force gradient across speed range. Anti-balance tab keeps control force manageable and prevents flutter. Electronic stability augmentation systems can supplement or replace mechanical anti-balance tabs.
exam_tip: Anti-balance tab moves WITH control to prevent flutter.
key_facts: All-moving tailplane characteristics
Hinge moment variation
Anti-balance tab simultaneous motion
Control force gradient maintenance
Flutter prevention method
Speed range force consistency
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.58.20

syllabus_desc: Describe the principle of operation of anti-balance tabs.
concept: Anti-balance tab is small auxiliary tab mechanically linked to main control surface (tailplane) via rigid linkage. When pilot pulls control input, both tailplane and anti-balance tab move together simultaneously. Tab creates additional aerodynamic moment opposing the control deflection, effectively increasing control force required. This increased force provides natural feel and prevents over-control. Rigidity of linkage ensures fixed relationship; tab cannot flutter independently. Mechanical advantage of linkage determines effectiveness. System maintains control force proportional to airspeed, preventing excessive response at high speed or insufficient response at low speed.
exam_tip: Anti-balance tab moves with control; increases force required.
key_facts: Mechanical linkage design
Simultaneous surface motion
Additional moment generation
Control force feedback
Fixed relationship maintenance
Speed-dependent force response
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.58.22

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of wing flaps.
concept: Wing flaps increase wing camber and effective area, increasing maximum coefficient of lift (CL-max), thereby reducing stall speed. Primary purpose: reduce landing distance by increasing lift at low airspeed, allowing shallower descent angle and shorter ground roll. Secondary benefits: increase drag for descent control, improve slow-speed handling. Extended flaps allow takeoff at shorter distance and from higher-altitude airfields. Flap extension increases drag significantly, so extended flaps limit cruise speed and endurance. Flaps are extension of trailing edge that rotate downward (and sometimes rearward) to change wing shape.
exam_tip: Flaps lower stall speed and increase drag for landing.
key_facts: Increased camber effect
CL-max increase
Stall speed reduction
Drag increase
Takeoff distance reduction
Landing distance reduction
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.58.24

syllabus_desc: Describe the principle of operation of wing flaps.
concept: Wing flaps are surfaces on trailing edge of wing, typically extending and rotating downward via mechanical linkage or hydraulic actuation. Simple designs: Plain flap (trailing edge rotates down), Split flap (undersurface rotates down). Complex designs: Slotted flap (extension and rotation with slot allowing airflow), Fowler flap (extends rearward and rotates, increasing wing area and camber significantly). Flap angle typically 0-40 degrees; initial 10-15 degrees provides best lift-to-drag ratio for takeoff; 30-40 degrees for landing. Actuation: manual lever, electrical switch, or fully automated. Cross-ribs and tracks guide motion.
exam_tip: Flaps extend and rotate to change wing shape.
key_facts: Plain vs slotted vs Fowler designs
Trailing edge rotation
Rearward extension capability
0-40 degree typical range
Hydraulic or electric actuation
Wing area increase
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.58.26

syllabus_desc: Define the four main forces acting in flight.
concept: Four main forces: (1) Weight (W) acts vertically downward through center of gravity; (2) Lift (L) acts perpendicular to relative airflow (wing); (3) Thrust (T) acts along engine axis forward; (4) Drag (D) acts parallel to relative airflow, opposing motion. In straight level flight: Lift equals Weight (L=W), Thrust equals Drag (T=D). These force pairs create equilibrium allowing sustained level flight without acceleration.
exam_tip: L=W and T=D in straight and level flight.
key_facts: Weight acts downward
Lift acts perpendicular to airflow
Thrust acts forward
Drag opposes motion
Equilibrium in level flight
Force balance requirement
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.60.2

syllabus_desc: For level flight describe how the four main forces change as IAS is varied.
concept: As IAS increases: drag increases (proportional to V-squared), lift increases proportionally, weight unchanged, so thrust must increase to balance increased drag. As IAS decreases: drag decreases, lift must increase to maintain altitude (angle of attack increases), thrust can decrease slightly. At minimum level flight speed, drag is minimum (best lift-to-drag ratio). Below minimum speed, further speed reduction requires excessive angle of attack, drag increases again, leading toward stall. Maximum level flight speed limited by available power/thrust. Aircraft naturally stabilizes at speed where thrust available equals power required at current power setting.
exam_tip: Drag increases with speed squared; minimum drag at L/D-max airspeed.
key_facts: Drag proportional to V²
Lift increase with speed
Angle of attack decrease at high speed
Minimum drag condition
Power required curve
Thrust available limit
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.60.4

syllabus_desc: Describe the pitching moments in flight, and how longitudinal stability is achieved.
concept: Pitching moments arise from: (1) Weight and lift (moment arm between CG and wing lift center); (2) Tail surface (horizontal stabilizer creates pitch moment). Aircraft has longitudinal stability when tail provides restoring moment opposite to any pitch disturbance. Horizontal stabilizer positioned aft of CG, producing downward force increasing with nose-down pitch (restoring). CG location critical: too far forward = excessive trim force, unstable; too far aft = insufficient restoring moment, unstable. Neutral point is CG location where stability is zero; margin of stability is distance between actual CG and neutral point. Positive static stability produces small oscillations after disturbance (stable). Short-period pitching oscillation damps out in stable aircraft.
exam_tip: Tail creates restoring moment; CG forward of neutral point = stable.
key_facts: Pitching moment components
Horizontal stabilizer restoring effect
CG-neutral point margin
Static stability requirement
Damping oscillation
CG envelope limits
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.60.6

syllabus_desc: Describe maximum and minimum level flight speed from PA/PR vs TAS graph.
concept: The Power Available (PA) vs Total Air Speed (TAS) graph shows the relationship between power required for level flight and airspeed. Maximum level flight speed occurs where Power Available (engine power curve) intersects Power Required at full throttle. Below this speed, power available exceeds power required. Minimum level flight speed is the speed at which Power Required is minimum (the bottom of the power required curve). At speeds below minimum speed in level flight, power required increases due to increased induced drag, and the aircraft cannot sustain level flight without descending.
exam_tip: Max speed = where PA curve meets engine power. Min speed = bottom of PR curve (stall speed or min drag speed). Exam expects both from graph.
key_facts: Maximum level flight speed at intersection of PA and PR
Occurs at full throttle
Below max speed, PA exceeds PR
Minimum level flight speed at PR minimum
Below min speed, PR increases (induced drag)
Aircraft cannot sustain level flight below min speed
Graph shows power vs airspeed relationship
Critical for cruise planning
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.60.8(a)

syllabus_desc: Describe maximum-range speed from PA/PR vs TAS graph.
concept: Maximum-range speed is the airspeed at which the aircraft can fly the farthest distance on a given quantity of fuel. This occurs where the ratio of TAS to Power Required (TAS/PR) is maximum, which graphically is where a line from the origin is tangent to the Power Required curve. At maximum-range speed, fuel burn per nautical mile is minimized. This speed is typically about 1.3–1.5 times the stall speed and is lower than maximum-endurance speed.
exam_tip: Max-range speed minimizes fuel per distance. Tangent from origin to PR curve. Use for ferry flights or when distance is priority.
key_facts: Maximum-range speed minimizes fuel burn per nautical mile
Found where tangent from origin touches PR curve
Ratio TAS/PR is maximum
Typically 1.3–1.5 × stall speed
Lower than maximum-endurance speed
Used for long-distance flights and ferry flights
Different from maximum-rate-of-climb speed
Critical for flight planning and fuel economy
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.60.8(b)

syllabus_desc: Describe maximum-endurance speed from PA/PR vs TAS graph.
concept: Maximum-endurance speed is the airspeed at which the aircraft can remain airborne for the longest time on a given quantity of fuel. This occurs where Power Required (fuel burn rate) is minimum, which is at the bottom of the Power Required curve. At maximum-endurance speed, power consumption per unit time is minimized, allowing maximum flight duration. This speed is typically about 1.1–1.3 times the stall speed and is lower than maximum-range speed.
exam_tip: Max-endurance speed minimizes power (fuel per time). Bottom of PR curve. Use for holding patterns, delay circuits, emergency situations.
key_facts: Maximum-endurance speed minimizes fuel burn per unit time
Occurs at minimum power required (bottom of PR curve)
Typically 1.1–1.3 × stall speed
Lower than maximum-range speed
Used for holding patterns and delay circuits
Used in emergency fuel situations
Different from maximum-range speed
Critical for flight planning under fuel constraints
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.60.8(c)

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic operational considerations which apply to flying an aeroplane for range, or endurance.
concept: For maximum range: fly at best-range speed (higher speed, acceptable fuel burn for distance covered), lean mixture for fuel efficiency, climb to altitude for lower fuel consumption, avoid headwind (or maximize tailwind), constant speed trim for efficiency. For maximum endurance: fly at best-endurance speed (lowest fuel consumption per hour), lean mixture, maintain economical cruise power, expect longest time aloft but shorter distance. Practical operations balance range/endurance with schedule, wind forecast, and fuel reserve requirements. Altitude affects both: higher altitude typically improves range/endurance efficiency (thinner air, less drag, less power required).
exam_tip: Range: maximize distance; endurance: maximize time. Lean mixture both.
key_facts: Best-range speed higher than best-endurance
Mixture leaning benefit
Altitude efficiency improvement
Wind effect on range
Power setting economy
Fuel reserve consideration
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.60.10

syllabus_desc: Using a diagram, show and name the forces acting in a steady climb.
concept: In steady climb: Weight (W) acts vertically downward; Lift (L) acts perpendicular to wing (at climb angle), component opposing weight is L-cos(climb-angle), component along flight path is L-sin(climb-angle); Thrust (T) acts along flight path forward; Drag (D) acts opposite flight path. For steady climb: Lift component up must exceed weight component, creating excess vertical force; Thrust must exceed Drag plus Weight component along descent. Excess power (Power available minus Power required) converts to rate of climb. Climb angle increases with excess thrust, decreases with weight.
exam_tip: Climbing forces: L > W vertically; T > D along flight path.
key_facts: Weight acts downward
Lift perpendicular to wing
Thrust along flight path
Drag opposes motion
Excess vertical force creates climb
Climb angle gradient
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.62.2

syllabus_desc: Describe maximum angle of climb and how it is achieved.
concept: Maximum angle of climb is the steepest pitch angle achievable, providing the greatest height gain per horizontal distance traveled. It is achieved at the airspeed where the excess power (PA minus PR) is maximum. This typically occurs at a lower airspeed than maximum rate of climb (around 1.2–1.3 × stall speed). Maximum angle of climb is critical for obstacle clearance after takeoff, especially from short airfields with obstacles nearby. Excess power × velocity = climb rate; maximizing excess power gives steepest angle.
exam_tip: Max angle of climb for obstacle clearance. Slower than max-rate-of-climb speed. Pitch up, reduce speed slightly from cruise.
key_facts: Steepest pitch angle for height per distance
Achieved at airspeed for maximum excess power
Lower airspeed than max-rate-of-climb
Typically 1.2–1.3 × stall speed
Critical for obstacle clearance
Used on takeoff near obstacles
Graph: maximum separation between PA and PR curves
Trade-off: slow airspeed, high climb angle
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.62.4(a)

syllabus_desc: Describe maximum rate of climb and how it is achieved.
concept: Maximum rate of climb is the greatest vertical speed achievable, providing the most height gain per unit time. It is achieved at the airspeed where the product of excess power and velocity (excess power × TAS = ROC) is maximum. This typically occurs at a higher airspeed than maximum angle of climb (around 1.5–1.7 × stall speed). On a power available vs power required graph, maximum rate of climb occurs where excess power (PA − PR) is greatest. Maximum rate of climb is used for en-route climbing to cruise altitude.
exam_tip: Max rate of climb maximizes feet per minute. Slightly faster than max-angle speed. Used for normal climbs to cruise.
key_facts: Greatest vertical speed (feet per minute)
Achieved where excess power is maximum
Higher airspeed than max-angle-of-climb
Typically 1.5–1.7 × stall speed
Excess power × velocity = climb rate
Used for routine en-route climbs
Graph: maximum vertical distance between PA and PR
Trade-off: faster airspeed, lower climb angle
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.62.4(b)

syllabus_desc: Describe cruise climb and its characteristics.
concept: Cruise climb is a shallow climb at a comfortable airspeed, typically around cruise speed or slightly below, maintaining good forward speed while gaining altitude. It is a compromise between maximum rate of climb and cruise speed, used for en-route climbing when obstacle clearance is not critical. Cruise climb is more efficient in terms of time to altitude compared to maximum angle of climb, and more comfortable for the aircraft and occupants than a steep climb. It allows the pilot to maintain reasonable airspeed and visibility while climbing to cruise altitude.
exam_tip: Cruise climb balances speed and climb rate. Slightly above best-rate airspeed. Comfortable long-duration climb.
key_facts: Shallow climb at near-cruise airspeed
Compromise between climb rate and airspeed
Used for en-route climbs
Faster and more efficient than max-angle-of-climb
Comfortable for aircraft and occupants
Maintains reasonable forward speed
Better visibility than steep climbs
Practical choice for routine operations
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.62.4(c)

syllabus_desc: Define the meaning of Vx (max angle) and Vy (max rate).
concept: Vx (maximum angle of climb speed): airspeed at which excess power (excess thrust) converted to climb provides steepest climb angle with ground. Occurs where tangent from origin touches excess power curve. Lower than Vy. Vx determines maximum altitude gain per horizontal distance, critical for obstacle clearance immediately after takeoff. Vy (maximum rate of climb speed): airspeed at which excess power provides steepest slope on power graph, giving maximum rate of altitude gain per unit time. Occurs where excess power is maximum. Higher than Vx. Vy determines quickest climb to altitude.
exam_tip: Vx = steepest climb angle; Vy = maximum climb rate.
key_facts: Vx excess power tangent from origin
Vy maximum excess power point
Vx lower than Vy
Angle vs rate distinction
Obstacle clearance application
Climb performance optimization
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.62.6

syllabus_desc: Using a PA/PR graph, show the derivation of maximum rate of climb speed.
concept: On Power Available vs Power Required graph: (1) Plot power required curve across airspeed range (shows power needed to maintain level flight at each speed). (2) Plot power available line (constant available power from engine at given RPM/setting). (3) Vertical separation between PA and PR curves represents excess power available for climb. (4) Maximum vertical separation occurs at Vy; at this point, excess power is greatest. (5) Vy is the airspeed corresponding to maximum vertical gap between PA and PR curves. Rate of climb equals (Excess Power) / Weight; Vy produces steepest climb rate. Graph shows that increasing power available (higher RPM) shifts PA line upward, increasing climb capability and raising Vy.
exam_tip: Vy = maximum vertical gap between PA and PR curves.
key_facts: PA and PR graph axes
Excess power vertical separation
Vy at maximum separation
Rate of climb calculation
Power increase effect
Airspeed optimization
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.62.8

syllabus_desc: Briefly explain the factors which affect climb performance.
concept: Climb factors: (1) Power available: higher power enables better climb (limited by engine power, ambient pressure, temperature). (2) Aircraft weight: heavier aircraft requires more power to climb (excess power decreases). (3) Density altitude: high altitude or hot day reduces engine power and air density (worse climb performance). (4) Configuration: flaps extended increases drag (worse climb), gear extended increases drag (worse climb). (5) Pitch attitude/airspeed: must maintain Vy for maximum rate or Vx for maximum angle. (6) Engine temperature: overheating limits climb power. (7) Fuel: heavy fuel load reduces rate of climb. Practical climbs balance these factors: climb to best density-altitude performance, lean mixture at altitude for power, monitor engine temperature, maintain efficient climb speed.
exam_tip: Density altitude most critical; climb at Vy for maximum rate.
key_facts: Engine power available critical
Weight effect on climb
Density altitude impact
Configuration drag effect
Speed optimization
Engine temperature limit
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.62.10

syllabus_desc: Identify and name the forces acting in a glide.
concept: In steady glide (no engine power): Weight (W) acts vertically downward; Lift (L) acts perpendicular to flight path; Drag (D) acts along flight path opposing motion. No thrust. For steady glide: Lift component perpendicular to descent equals weight component normal to descent path; Weight component along descent path equals drag component along descent. Glide angle is shallow when lift/drag ratio is high. Steeper glide occurs when L/D decreases (extended flaps or higher drag).
exam_tip: Glide: L perpendicular to path, W component balances drag.
key_facts: Weight acts downward
Lift perpendicular to flight path
Drag along flight path
No engine thrust
L/D determines glide angle
Descent rate relationship
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.64.2

syllabus_desc: Explain how the forces in a glide become modified in a constant speed power on descent.
concept: In constant-speed power-on descent (descending cruise): Thrust added along flight path reduces requirement for gravity to provide descent force. Engine provides partial support of weight. At shallow descent angle: Lift still balances most of weight (slightly less); Thrust plus small weight component down balance Drag. Pilot controls descent rate by varying power: more power reduces descent rate, less power increases descent rate. In steady descent: rate constant, forces balanced about descent path. Aircraft maintains constant airspeed through pitch control; power adjustment controls descent rate without changing speed.
exam_tip: Power-on descent: thrust reduces required descent angle.
key_facts: Thrust along flight path
Shallow descent angle
Lift still large component
Power controls descent rate
Pitch controls airspeed
Constant speed descent
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.64.4

syllabus_desc: Explain how the lift/drag ratio determines a constant speed glide angle.
concept: Lift/Drag ratio (L/D or efficiency) equals glide ratio. In no-power glide at steady descent: Weight component along flight path (W-sin-glide-angle) equals Drag. Weight component perpendicular to path equals Lift. Glide angle determined by: tan(glide-angle) = Drag / Lift = 1 / (L/D). High L/D (efficient wing) produces shallow glide angle and long distance from given altitude. Low L/D (draggy configuration like extended flaps) produces steep glide angle. Example: L/D of 10 means for every 10 units forward, aircraft descends 1 unit. Maximum L/D occurs at angle of attack providing best efficiency, typically near cruise speed with flaps retracted.
exam_tip: Glide angle = inverse of L/D ratio.
key_facts: L/D ratio defines glide angle
Tan(glide-angle) = 1/L/D
High L/D shallow glide
Low L/D steep glide
Maximum L/D efficiency point
Distance from altitude calculation
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.64.6

syllabus_desc: Briefly explain the effects of weight, IAS, wind, and flap extension on the glide angle.
concept: Weight: heavier aircraft glides at same angle but at higher airspeed; weight doesn't change glide angle, only glide speed. IAS: glide angle unchanged by changing speed; L/D ratio remains same at each angle of attack, so glide angle fixed. Wind: headwind shortens glide distance (same angle, slower ground speed), tailwind extends distance; wind doesn't change glide angle but changes ground distance. Flap extension: increases drag, lowers L/D, increases glide angle (steeper), reduces glide distance. Flaps should be retracted initially in power-off descent to maximize glide distance, extended only for landing approach.
exam_tip: Flaps steepen glide; wind changes ground distance not angle.
key_facts: Weight affects glide speed not angle
IAS doesn't change L/D
Wind affects ground distance
Flaps increase drag and angle
Retract flaps for distance
Glide angle is fixed ratio
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.64.8

syllabus_desc: Define centripetal force.
concept: Centripetal force is net inward force required to change aircraft direction and maintain circular path. In a level turn, horizontal component of lift provides centripetal force (toward center of turn). Centripetal force = (Weight × Velocity-squared) / (Gravity × Radius). Larger centripetal force needed for: higher speed (increases with V-squared), tighter turn radius (smaller R increases force), or higher load factor. Aircraft cannot turn without centripetal force; magnitude of force determines turn rate and radius.
exam_tip: Centripetal force = inward force for circular path.
key_facts: Net inward force definition
Lift horizontal component source
Proportional to V-squared
Inverse to radius
Load factor relationship
Turn geometry dependence
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.66.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the turning force (centripetal component of lift in a turn).
concept: In a turn, the lift force has two components: (1) vertical component = weight (to balance weight and maintain altitude), and (2) horizontal component (toward center of turn) = centripetal force, which causes the change in direction. This horizontal component is the turning force. The greater the bank angle, the larger the horizontal (centripetal) component of lift. The turning force is proportional to bank angle and the total lift force: turning force = lift × sin(bank angle). This is why steeper banks result in tighter turns.
exam_tip: Turning force is horizontal component of lift. Bank angle splits lift into vertical (up) and horizontal (turning).
key_facts: Horizontal component of lift in a turn
Causes aircraft to change direction
Proportional to bank angle
Turning force = lift × sin(bank angle)
Steeper bank = greater turning force
Centripetal acceleration toward turn center
Provides centripetal acceleration
Critical for coordinated flight
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.66.4(a)

syllabus_desc: Describe the force opposing weight (vertical component of lift in a turn).
concept: In a turn, the vertical component of lift must equal the aircraft's weight to maintain altitude and prevent descent or climb. As the aircraft banks, the total lift force is tilted, and only the vertical component opposes weight. The vertical component of lift = lift × cos(bank angle). In a level turn, this vertical component must remain equal to weight. To maintain altitude during a turn, total lift must increase to compensate for the tilted lift vector. The greater the bank angle, the larger the ratio of total lift to weight required to maintain level flight.
exam_tip: Vertical lift component = weight (to hold altitude). At 60° bank, need 2× lift to stay level. Stall speed increases in turns.
key_facts: Vertical component of lift in a turn
Must equal weight to maintain altitude
Vertical lift = lift × cos(bank angle)
Steeper bank requires greater total lift
Prevents descent/climb during turn
Load factor increases with bank angle
Stall speed increases in turns
Critical for level-turn performance
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.66.4(b)

syllabus_desc: Define load factor 'g'.
concept: Load factor (g) is ratio of total lift to weight in maneuvering flight: g = Lift / Weight. In level flight, g = 1. In a 20° bank turn, g = 1 / cos(20°) ≈ 1.06. In a 60° bank turn, g ≈ 2. Load factor increases rapidly with bank angle (exponential relationship). High load factor increases stall speed: Vs-turn = Vs-level × √g. Structural limit typically 3-6g for general aviation aircraft; aerobatic aircraft 9-10g. Pilot experiences apparent weight increase equal to g; 2g feels like twice normal weight. Prolonged high-g maneuvers cause pilot fatigue and G-induced Loss Of Consciousness (G-LOC).
exam_tip: Load factor g = Lift/Weight; increases exponentially with bank.
key_facts: g = 1 in level flight
g = 1/cos(bank) formula
60° bank ≈ 2g
Stall speed increases √g
Structural limits typical
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.66.6

syllabus_desc: In a level turn, state the relationship between bank angle and lift, drag, and load factor.
concept: Level turn relationships: (1) Bank angle increases: centripetal component of lift increases (proportional to sin-bank); vertical component decreases (proportional to cos-bank). (2) Total lift must increase to maintain vertical component = weight (L = W / cos-bank), so required lift increases with bank. (3) Increased lift requires increased angle of attack, which increases drag significantly (drag ∝ angle-of-attack-squared). (4) Load factor g = 1 / cos(bank-angle). At 0° bank g=1; at 20° bank g≈1.06; at 45° bank g≈1.41; at 60° bank g≈2.0. Steeper bank dramatically increases both lift requirement and drag, requiring more engine power to maintain altitude and airspeed.
exam_tip: Bank increase: lift increases by 1/cos(angle), load factor rises.
key_facts: Lift vertical component must equal weight
Total lift = W/cos(bank)
Drag increases with angle of attack
Load factor exponential increase
Power requirement increases
Stall speed increases √g
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.66.8

syllabus_desc: Describe the relationship between airspeed and turn radius and rate of turn.
concept: Turn radius is directly proportional to the square of airspeed (R ∝ V²). Doubling airspeed quadruples turn radius. This means high-speed aircraft (jets) have much larger turn radii than slow aircraft. Rate of turn is inversely proportional to airspeed (ω ∝ 1/V). A slower aircraft can turn more sharply and complete a turn in less time than a faster aircraft at the same bank angle. For a given bank angle, decreasing speed increases rate of turn and decreases turn radius, while increasing speed decreases rate of turn and increases turn radius.
exam_tip: Airspeed affects turn performance: slow = tight turn, fast = shallow turn. High-speed aircraft need gentle bank angles for air traffic.
key_facts: Turn radius proportional to airspeed squared (R ∝ V²)
High airspeed = large turn radius
Low airspeed = small turn radius
Rate of turn inversely proportional to airspeed
Slow aircraft turn sharply and quickly
Fast aircraft turn gently and slowly
Critical for air traffic control procedures
Fundamental to coordinated flight
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.66.10(a)

syllabus_desc: Describe the relationship between bank angle and turn radius and rate of turn.
concept: Turn radius is inversely proportional to the tangent of bank angle (R ∝ 1/tan(Φ)). Increasing bank angle decreases turn radius. A 30° bank gives a larger radius than a 20° bank. Rate of turn is proportional to the tangent of bank angle (ω ∝ tan(Φ)). Increasing bank angle increases rate of turn. A 60° bank produces a much tighter turn (smaller radius, faster rate) than a 20° bank. The relationship is non-linear; beyond 30° bank, rate of turn increases significantly. Maximum practical bank angle in level flight is typically 25–30° for comfort; combat/aerobatic aircraft use 60°+.
exam_tip: Bank angle determines sharpness of turn: steep bank = tight turn, shallow bank = wide turn. Rate of turn increases with bank.
key_facts: Turn radius inversely proportional to tan(bank angle)
Steep bank = small turn radius
Shallow bank = large turn radius
Rate of turn proportional to tan(bank angle)
Steeper bank = faster rate of turn
Relationship is non-linear above 30°
Practical limit ~25–30° for normal operations
Critical for ATC procedure compliance
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.66.10(b)

syllabus_desc: Describe a rate 1 turn, and a rule-of-thumb method of calculating the bank angle required.
concept: Rate 1 turn (also standard rate turn): turn rate of 3 degrees per second, completing 360° turn in 2 minutes. Used in instrument flying for standardized turn. Rule-of-thumb for bank angle required for rate 1: Bank angle (degrees) ≈ (Airspeed-in-knots / 10) + 5. Example: at 100 knots, bank angle ≈ (100/10) + 5 = 15°; at 200 knots, bank angle ≈ 20 + 5 = 25°. This formula produces approximately 3°/second turn rate. For standard rate turns, instrument cross-check shows rate of heading change on heading indicator; pilot adjusts bank to maintain rate 1.
exam_tip: Rate 1 turn: 3°/sec. Bank ≈ (IAS÷10) + 5 for rate 1.
key_facts: Rate 1 = 3°/second turn
360° in 2 minutes
Rule-of-thumb formula
IAS/10 + 5 degrees bank
Instrument turn coordination
Standard rate definition
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.66.12

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of bank on rate of climb in a climbing turn, and the tendency to 'overbank'.
concept: In climbing turn: banking increases load factor (g = 1/cos-bank), requiring increased lift to maintain both altitude and turn. Increased lift requires increased angle of attack, which increases drag significantly. Increased drag reduces available excess power for climb. Result: rate of climb decreases in turn compared to straight climb at same power. Steeper bank produces greater climb reduction. Overbanking tendency: if pilot maintains pitch attitude (constant angle of attack) through a climbing turn without increasing back pressure, the vertical component of lift decreases with increasing bank, causing aircraft to descend rather than climb. To maintain constant climb rate in turn, pilot must increase back pressure (increase angle of attack) as bank increases. Failure to increase back pressure results in gradual descent (overbanking).
exam_tip: Banking reduces climb rate; increase back pressure to prevent descent.
key_facts: Increased load factor in turn
Drag increase reduces climb
Pitch attitude maintenance needed
Back pressure requirement
Overbanking descent risk
Bank angle climb effect
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.66.14

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of bank on rate of descent in a descending turn, and the tendency to 'underbank'.
concept: In descending turn: banking decreases vertical component of lift (L-cos-bank), requiring increased back pressure to maintain desired descent rate (maintain altitude loss). If pilot maintains pitch attitude without increasing back pressure during turn, vertical lift component decreases, causing descent rate to decrease (shallower glide). Result: aircraft descends slower than intended (tends to climb relative to descent plan). Underbanking tendency: to maintain constant descent rate in turn, pilot must increase back pressure (increase angle of attack and lift) to compensate for reduced vertical component from banking. Failure to increase back pressure results in shallower descent than desired. Practical: in approach turn to descend at constant rate, pilot must increase back pressure as bank increases.
exam_tip: Banking reduces descent rate; increase back pressure for constant descent.
key_facts: Banking decreases vertical lift
Descent rate decreases in turn
Pitch attitude adjustment needed
Back pressure requirement
Underbanking descent reduction
Bank effect on descent
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.66.16

syllabus_desc: Describe disruption of streamline flow over upper surface of aerofoil at stall.
concept: At low angles of attack, air flows smoothly over the aerofoil surface following the contour (streamline or laminar flow). As angle of attack increases, the air is forced to follow an increasingly sharp curve near the leading edge. Beyond the critical angle of attack (approximately 15–20° for a typical Cessna wing), the air cannot follow the surface curve and separates from the upper surface, creating a turbulent wake and eddy patterns. This flow separation disrupts the organized low-pressure region that normally generates lift on the upper surface, causing lift to collapse and drag to increase dramatically.
exam_tip: Flow separation is the key to stall. Air can't follow curved surface at high AOA. Turbulent wake behind wing.
key_facts: Streamline flow at low angles of attack
Sharp surface curvature at high angle of attack
Flow cannot follow surface beyond critical AOA
Boundary layer separates from upper surface
Turbulent wake and eddy formation
Loss of organized pressure distribution
Critical angle of attack ~15–20° (typical wing)
Occurs on upper surface first, then lower
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.68.2(a)

syllabus_desc: Describe reduction of lift and increase of drag during stall.
concept: When the boundary layer separates due to flow disruption, the low-pressure region on the upper surface is destroyed, so the net pressure difference across the aerofoil decreases sharply, causing lift to fall (often dropping to 50% or less of pre-stall value). Simultaneously, the turbulent wake behind the separated flow creates a large pressure drag component (form drag), which increases dramatically. The combination of reduced lift and increased drag means the aircraft can no longer maintain altitude and must descend. This is the characteristic 'mushing' or 'sinking' feeling of a stall. Drag can double or triple at stall angle of attack.
exam_tip: Stall = lift collapse + drag increase. Wing no longer generates lift. Aircraft descends uncontrollably.
key_facts: Net pressure difference across aerofoil decreases
Lift reduces to ~50% or less
Turbulent wake creates form drag
Total drag increases dramatically (2–3×)
Loss of lift cause descent
Aircraft 'mushes' downward
Occurs suddenly beyond critical angle of attack
Recovery requires reduction of angle of attack
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.68.2(b)

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms of a developing stall.
concept: Early stall warning signs: (1) aircraft feels 'mushy' (reduced control effectiveness, especially aileron response); (2) pitch control becomes heavy, requiring increased back pressure; (3) stall warning horn/light activates (if equipped), typically 5-10 knots before stall; (4) airframe vibration or buffeting as separated flow interacts with fuselage; (5) turn tendency (one wing stalls before other); (6) descent rate increases despite level attitude; (7) airspeed approaches stall speed; (8) rate of descent on altimeter noticeably increases. In gliding turn, stall may occur without obvious warning. Recognition essential to initiate immediate recovery.
exam_tip: Buffeting, mushy controls, and stall horn warn of developing stall.
key_facts: Mushy control feeling
Heavy pitch control
Stall warning horn/light
Airframe vibration/buffeting
Turn tendency development
Increased descent rate
Airspeed approaching limit
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.68.4

syllabus_desc: Explain that stall is associated with a particular angle of attack, not a particular airspeed.
concept: The stall occurs when the angle of attack exceeds the critical angle of attack (approximately 15–20° for a typical wing), regardless of the aircraft's airspeed. A loaded aircraft with a higher weight stalls at a higher airspeed than a light aircraft, but both stall at the same critical angle of attack. An aircraft in a turn stalls at a higher airspeed than in level flight due to increased load factor, but again at the same critical angle of attack. This is why relying on airspeed alone to avoid stall is dangerous. Pilots must understand and control angle of attack through pitch control and airspeed management.
exam_tip: Stall = critical angle of attack reached, not low speed. Loaded aircraft stall at higher airspeed. Pitch control = AOA control.
key_facts: Stall occurs at critical angle of attack (~15–20°)
Independent of aircraft weight
Independent of airspeed
Critical AOA is same for loaded/light aircraft
Heavier aircraft stall at higher airspeed
In turns, stall speed increases but AOA is same
Angle of attack is the critical variable
Airspeed is not a reliable stall indicator
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.68.6(a)

syllabus_desc: Explain that reduction of angle of attack is critical to stall recovery.
concept: To recover from a stall, the pilot must immediately reduce the angle of attack below the critical angle by pushing the control column forward (lowering the nose). This restores streamline flow over the aerofoil, allowing lift to be re-established. Continuing to pull back on the controls keeps the aircraft in the stalled condition and may result in a spin. Once the angle of attack is reduced and airflow is restored, the pilot can then apply power and raise the nose to resume normal flight. Rapid recovery of airspeed is secondary to recovery of lift through AOA reduction.
exam_tip: Stall recovery: push nose down first (reduce AOA). Restore lift before worrying about altitude. Recovery is nose-down reflex.
key_facts: Reduce angle of attack below critical angle
Lower nose by pushing control column forward
Restores streamline flow over wing
Re-establishes lift generation
Must occur immediately for safe recovery
Pulling back prolongs/deepens stall
Power is secondary to AOA reduction
Recovery results in altitude loss
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.68.6(b)

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of load factor on stall speed.
concept: Load factor (G) is the ratio of total lift to aircraft weight. In level flight, load factor = 1 G. In a turn, load factor increases: at 30° bank, load factor ≈ 1.15 G; at 60° bank, load factor ≈ 2 G. Stall speed increases with the square root of load factor (Vs = Vs₀ × √n, where n is load factor). A 2 G maneuver increases stall speed by √2 ≈ 1.41 times. This is why stall speed in turns is much higher than in level flight, and why aggressive maneuvers are dangerous at low speed.
exam_tip: Load factor increases stall speed by √n factor. 2 G turn: stall speed 1.41× level-flight value. Bank angle = higher stall speed.
key_facts: Load factor = total lift / weight
1 G in level flight
Increases with bank angle
2 G at ~60° bank
Stall speed ∝ √load factor
Stall speed increases in turns
Aggressive maneuvers increase stall speed
Critical safety consideration
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.68.8(a)

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of aircraft weight on stall speed.
concept: Heavier aircraft have higher stall speeds than lighter aircraft, following the relationship: stall speed ∝ √weight. An aircraft at maximum gross weight stalls at a higher airspeed than the same aircraft at half weight. This is because higher weight requires higher lift, which (at a given angle of attack) requires higher airspeed to generate. The critical angle of attack does not change with weight, but the airspeed needed to generate sufficient lift to balance that weight increases with weight. Weight affects the airspeed at which stall occurs but not the angle of attack at which it occurs.
exam_tip: Heavier aircraft = higher stall speed. Stall speed ∝ √weight. Full fuel = higher stall speed. Affects all speeds.
key_facts: Higher weight = higher stall speed
Stall speed ∝ √weight
Heavier aircraft require higher lift
Lift = ½ρV²SCₗ, so higher V needed
Critical angle of attack unchanged
Gross weight stall speed > empty weight stall speed
Affects approach and landing speeds
Full fuel increases weight and stall speed
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.68.8(b)

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of altitude on stall speed.
concept: Stall speed (true airspeed, TAS) is independent of altitude. The critical angle of attack is a geometric property of the wing and is reached at the same TAS regardless of altitude. However, stall speed expressed as indicated airspeed (IAS) increases slightly with altitude because the air density is lower. At sea level, IAS ≈ TAS, but at altitude, TAS > IAS, so the same critical angle of attack is reached at a slightly higher IAS. In practical flight, stall speed is published as IAS and is essentially constant across the aircraft's operating altitude range (2–3% variation). Pilots should note that TAS increases with altitude for the same IAS, making true airspeed higher at altitude for any given maneuver.
exam_tip: True stall speed (TAS) is constant. Indicated stall speed is nearly constant. At altitude, TAS > IAS for same indicated airspeed.
key_facts: Stall speed (TAS) is altitude-independent
Critical angle of attack is geometric property
Same TAS stall at sea level or altitude
IAS stall speed is nearly constant (2–3% variation)
At altitude: TAS > IAS
Density altitude affects density but not stall AOA
Published stall speeds are IAS
Practical effect is minor for small aircraft
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.68.8(c)

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of power on stall speed.
concept: High power decreases stall speed slightly. When power is applied, the slipstream accelerates air over the wings, increasing dynamic pressure and lift at any given airspeed. This allows a lower airspeed (and lower angle of attack) to generate the same lift. Conversely, power-off (glide) stall speed is higher because no slipstream benefit is available. The effect is most noticeable in high-powered aircraft with large propeller slipstreams. Power-off stall speed can be 5–10% higher than power-on stall speed depending on aircraft design. This is why power-off approaches and landings are more critical than powered operations.
exam_tip: Power on = lower stall speed (slipstream effect). Power off = higher stall speed. Landing more critical than powered flight.
key_facts: Power-on reduces stall speed
Slipstream increases airflow over wing
Slipstream increases lift
Power-off stall speed is higher
Effect is 5–10% in high-powered aircraft
High propeller slipstream = bigger effect
Power affects angle of attack needed
Critical for takeoff and landing operations
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.68.8(d)

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of flap extension on stall speed.
concept: Flap extension significantly reduces stall speed. Flaps increase wing camber and effectively increase the lift coefficient (Cₗ), allowing the aircraft to generate the required lift at a lower airspeed. A typical aircraft might have a clean stall speed of 50 knots (IAS) but stall speed of 40 knots (IAS) with full flaps. The stall speed reduction is typically 10–20% depending on flap design and configuration. Flap deflection also increases drag and reduces the angle of attack needed to reach critical conditions. Pilots extend flaps during approach and landing to reduce stall speed and improve controllability at low speeds.
exam_tip: Flaps reduce stall speed significantly. Increased camber = higher Cₗ = lower speed needed for same lift. Landing: full flaps = slower stall.
key_facts: Flap extension increases wing camber
Increases lift coefficient (Cₗ)
Allows lower airspeed for same lift
Stall speed reduction 10–20%
Full flaps: stall speed ~20% lower
Flap drag also increases
Used for approach and landing
Improves low-speed handling
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.68.8(e)

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of damage, ice, frost, or contamination on stall speed.
concept: Damage, ice, frost, or any contamination of the wing surface increases stall speed. Ice or frost roughens the leading edge, disrupting smooth boundary layer flow and causing premature separation. Wing damage (wrinkles, dents) or contamination (dirt, bugs) similarly disrupts flow. These surface irregularities reduce the effective lift coefficient at a given angle of attack, requiring a higher airspeed to generate sufficient lift. Ice accumulation also changes the wing shape, further reducing aerodynamic efficiency. Stall speed can increase by 10–30% with moderate ice/contamination, and severely iced wings may stall at much higher speeds. This is a critical safety issue: aircraft with wing contamination are not airworthy.
exam_tip: Ice, frost, damage increase stall speed. Rough surface disrupts flow. Iced wing = much higher stall speed. Do not fly if contaminated.
key_facts: Ice/frost disrupts boundary layer flow
Premature flow separation
Reduces lift coefficient (Cₗ)
Requires higher airspeed for same lift
Stall speed increase 10–30% typical
Severe ice can double stall speed
Wing damage (wrinkles, dents) increases stall speed
Aircraft unairworthy if wing contaminated
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.68.8(f)

syllabus_desc: Describe the possible consequences of using ailerons near, during and in the recovery from a stall.
concept: Near stall: ailerons control becomes increasingly ineffective (reduced airflow over control surfaces); aileron input may cause adverse yaw, potentially inducing wing drop. During stall: ailerons largely ineffective (separated flow); aileron deflection on dropping wing may increase that wing's stall angle, deepening stall on that wing. Attempting to raise dropped wing with aileron typically drops opposite wing or causes spin. Recovery: ailerons should not be used during stall recovery; maintain neutral aileron during pitch-down recovery. Use rudder only to maintain coordination after recovery. Premature aileron application during recovery risks deepening stall or inducing spin.
exam_tip: Don't use ailerons during stall; use neutral aileron and rudder.
key_facts: Near-stall aileron effectiveness loss
Adverse yaw from aileron near stall
Aileron ineffective during stall
Dropping wing aggravation
Spin induction risk
Recovery procedure neutral aileron
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.68.10

syllabus_desc: Define the term autorotation and the conditions leading to it.
concept: Autorotation: self-sustaining rolling motion (rotation about longitudinal axis) during stalled flight condition. Occurs when: (1) one wing stalls while other wing maintains some lift (asymmetric stall); (2) airflow over aileron already stalled wing is too disturbed to respond effectively to aileron input; (3) control attempts to raise dropping wing with aileron paradoxically deepen that wing's stall; (4) unequal stall conditions persist and rolling motion accelerates. Autorotation typically leads to incipient or developed spin. Prevented by: maintaining wings level near stall, neutral ailerons during stall condition, immediate and full pitch-down recovery, avoiding control inputs that induce differential stall.
exam_tip: Autorotation: self-sustaining roll from asymmetric stall.
key_facts: Asymmetric stall condition
Aileron control ineffectiveness
Rolling moment development
Differential stall deepening
Spin development risk
Prevention through neutral controls
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.68.12

syllabus_desc: Describe the stalled condition of flight in a spin.
concept: A spin is an autorotational descent in which the aircraft is flying in a stalled condition (beyond the critical angle of attack on at least one wing). The stall disrupts lift generation and causes the aircraft to descend. The stall is maintained throughout the spin because the high angle of attack is continuously re-established by the rotational motion and the unrecovered stalled condition. The wing on the inside of the turn stalls more deeply than the outer wing due to the difference in relative airspeed across the wings. This asymmetric stall causes the aircraft to rotate about its vertical axis. The spin is not a recovery from stall; rather, it is a sustained stalled descent with autorotation.
exam_tip: Spin = sustained stall condition. Inside wing more deeply stalled. Autorotation continues stall. Not spiral dive.
key_facts: Aircraft is in stalled condition
Angle of attack beyond critical angle
Stall is maintained throughout spin
One or both wings fully stalled
Inside wing more deeply stalled
Asymmetric stall causes rotation
Not a spiral dive (different recovery)
Altitude loss accelerates in spin
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.68.14(a)

syllabus_desc: Describe the simultaneous motion about three axes in a spin.
concept: During a spin, the aircraft rotates simultaneously about all three axes: (1) pitch (rotation nose-down, nose-up about the lateral/Y-axis), (2) roll (rotation about the longitudinal/X-axis, wings rolling down and up), and (3) yaw (rotation about the vertical/Z-axis, nose rotating left or right). The primary motion is yaw (rotation about vertical axis), but the pitch and roll axes are also in motion. The aircraft descends steeply while rotating.
