This three-axis rotation is what distinguishes a spin from a simple dive (pitch only) or a barrel roll (pitch and roll primarily). The simultaneous motion about three axes makes recovery complex and requires specific inputs to break the spin.
exam_tip: Spin = rotation about all three axes. Primary motion is yaw (rotation about vertical). Pitch/roll also present. Complex motion.
key_facts: Pitch rotation (nose-down/up) about Y-axis
Roll rotation (wings rolling) about X-axis
Yaw rotation (nose rotating) about Z-axis
Primary motion is yaw about vertical axis
Simultaneous motion on all axes
Distinguishes spin from dive or roll
Aircraft descends steeply while rotating
Makes recovery maneuver essential
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.68.14(b)

syllabus_desc: Describe the high rate of descent at low airspeed in a spin.
concept: During a spin, the aircraft descends very rapidly (often 200–500 ft/min or more in a developed spin) while the airspeed remains relatively low (often below stall speed due to the stalled condition). This combination of high descent rate and low airspeed is characteristic of the spin and makes altitude loss alarming. A typical light aircraft might lose 500 feet per turn in a developed spin, meaning the spin can consume altitude very quickly. The low airspeed results from the stalled condition and the high descent rate results from the loss of lift and the steep pitch angle of the descent. The combination makes spin recovery and training critical for safety, as spinning into terrain is a hazard at low altitudes.
exam_tip: Spin = steep descent, low airspeed. Loses ~500 ft/turn in small aircraft. Dangerous near ground. Recovery immediate and critical.
key_facts: High vertical descent rate (200–500+ ft/min)
Low airspeed (below stall speed)
Steep pitch angle downward
Rotating descent, not vertical descent
Altitude loss 300–500 ft per turn typical
Low altitude spin is fatal (no recovery room)
Recovery becomes more difficult deeper in spin
Prevention (never stall turning) critical
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.68.14(c)

syllabus_desc: Describe the difference between spin and spiral dive.
concept: A spin is a stalled autorotational descent with low airspeed and high descent rate. A spiral dive is a steep, descending, spiraling flight path with the aircraft NOT stalled, at high airspeed, and not autorotating; instead, it results from over-banking in a turn. In a spin, the inside wing is stalled and generates less lift; in a spiral dive, both wings have full lift and are flying normally. Recovery from a spin requires reducing angle of attack (nose down, reduce power) and stopping rotation (neutralize ailerons, apply opposite rudder). Recovery from a spiral dive requires reducing power and gently raising the nose (increasing back-pressure) without stalling. The airspeed indicator is a useful (but not perfect) differentiator: spin = slow, spiral = fast. However, disorientation may prevent accurate instrument reading.
exam_tip: Spin = stalled, slow, rotating descent. Spiral = fast, steep, non-stalled descent. Recovery differs. Instruments may help distinguish.
key_facts: Spin: stalled condition, low airspeed
Spin: autorotation about vertical axis
Spiral: not stalled, high airspeed
Spiral: steep descent, not rotating
Spin recovery: reduce AOA, stop rotation
Spiral recovery: reduce power, raise nose
Spin: both wings not generating full lift
Spiral: both wings generating full lift
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.68.14(d)

syllabus_desc: State what actions can be taken to avoid a spin.
concept: Spin avoidance: (1) maintain adequate airspeed; recognize stall warning signs and recover immediately. (2) Maintain wings level in all maneuvers, especially turns near stall speed. (3) Never use ailerons during stall; maintain neutral aileron control. (4) Avoid skidding turns; coordinate turn with proper rudder (avoid slip/skid). (5) Avoid cross-control situation (e.g., aileron one direction, rudder opposite direction). (6) Never increase back pressure during stall recovery. (7) Avoid steep turns at low altitude near stall speed. (8) Maintain awareness of airspeed during approach/landing; execute go-around if airspeed drops below safe threshold.
exam_tip: Maintain airspeed, wings level, neutral ailerons during stall.
key_facts: Adequate airspeed maintenance
Stall recognition and recovery
Wings level critical
Neutral aileron during stall
Coordinated flight
No cross-control inputs
Forward stick recovery
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.68.16

syllabus_desc: Explain the 'standard' recovery action from a developed spin.
concept: Standard spin recovery procedure (may vary by aircraft): (1) Determine spin direction (observe turn coordinator or magnetic compass, or feel yaw movement). (2) Apply full rudder opposite spin direction (if spinning right, apply full left rudder). (3) Immediately apply full forward control column (pitch down completely) to break stall and reduce angle of attack below stall angle. (4) Hold forward stick and opposite rudder until spin stops (rotation ceases) and airspeed begins increasing. (5) Once rotation stops, neutralize rudder and gradually recover from resulting dive with gentle back pressure. (6) Return to level flight. Timing critical: forward stick must be applied with opposite rudder; delaying either control extends recovery time and altitude loss. Recovery may require 500-1000 feet altitude depending on spin entry altitude.
exam_tip: Opposite rudder + forward stick breaks spin rotation.
key_facts: Determine spin direction
Full opposite rudder
Full forward stick pitch-down
Hold controls until rotation stops
Neutralize rudder after rotation stops
Gradual recovery to level flight
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.68.18

syllabus_desc: Identify and explain the basic function of the major components of a conventional airframe.
concept: Major components: (1) Fuselage: main body providing structure and housing crew, cargo, systems. (2) Wings: primary lift-generating surfaces. (3) Tail surfaces: horizontal stabilizer (pitch control/stability), vertical stabilizer (yaw stability), control surfaces (elevator, rudder). (4) Landing gear: supports aircraft on ground, absorbs landing impact. (5) Engine/powerplant: provides thrust. (6) Control systems: transmit pilot inputs to control surfaces. Conventional airframe uses monocoque or semi-monocoque construction with skin providing primary stress resistance. Main loads: bending (wings in flight), shear, torsion.
exam_tip: Fuselage, wings, tail, gear, engine, controls are main components.
key_facts: Fuselage main structure
Wings generate lift
Tail surfaces stabilize and control
Landing gear support
Engine provides thrust
Control systems operate surfaces
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.70.2

syllabus_desc: Describe load on ground and the function of spars and struts.
concept: On the ground, the aircraft structure must support its own weight (static load) through the landing gear and fuselage. The wings are subject to bending loads from their own weight and from concentrated loads (fuel, engines, payloads). The primary structural members are the spars, which are beam-like structures (main spar and auxiliary spars) running spanwise through the wing and fuselage. Spars carry bending loads and shear forces. Struts (external or internal bracing members) provide additional support and transfer wing bending loads back to the fuselage. In some designs, external wing struts prevent wing flutter and reduce bending moments. The fuselage must resist torsion and bending from wing loads transmitted to it.
exam_tip: Ground load = weight support. Spars carry bending. Struts reduce bending moment on wings. Fuselage transmits loads.
key_facts: Aircraft weight is static load on ground
Wings must support own weight plus loads
Spars are primary bending members
Main and auxiliary spars
Struts provide bracing and support
External/internal struts reduce bending
Fuselage carries wing-to-fuselage loads
Torsion and bending stresses critical
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.70.4(a)

syllabus_desc: Describe load in the air and the function of spars and struts.
concept: In flight, aerodynamic forces create much larger loads than gravity alone. The wing generates lift, which creates upward loads that are translated to bending moments in the spars and fuselage. In turns or during maneuvers, load factor multiplies these aerodynamic loads (at 2 G, all loads double). The spars and wing structure must resist these dynamic bending, torsion, and shear stresses. Struts and bracing reduce the bending moment by distributing loads more efficiently, reducing the required thickness and weight of spars. High-performance aircraft often use more bracing (flying wires, struts) to reduce structural weight. The fuselage must absorb the combined wing loads (lift and side loads from yaw) and transmit them safely to the landing gear.
exam_tip: Air load = aerodynamic forces × load factor. Spars resist bending from lift. Struts distribute loads. Maneuvers increase loads.
key_facts: Aerodynamic lift creates upward loads
Load factor multiplies forces in turns
Bending, torsion, and shear stresses
Spars carry primary bending loads
Struts reduce bending moments
Bracing members distribute loads
Reduces required spar thickness/weight
Critical for high-G maneuvers
source: Kermode Mechanics of Flight, Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.70.4(b)

syllabus_desc: Describe precautions for structural integrity with aluminium construction.
concept: Aluminium is a common aircraft material due to its high strength-to-weight ratio, corrosion resistance, and workability. Precautions for aluminium structural integrity include: (1) prevent corrosion through painting, anodizing, and drainage holes (water/salt damage); (2) prevent stress-corrosion cracking (fatigue cracks initiate at corrosion pits); (3) regular inspection for cracks, dents, wrinkles (indicate overstress or corrosion); (4) protect from galvanic corrosion when in contact with dissimilar metals; (5) avoid moisture traps and ensure proper ventilation; (6) maintain protective coatings. Dents in aluminium can reduce fatigue strength even if small, so aircraft-quality repairs are essential.
exam_tip: Aluminium needs protection from corrosion and fatigue. Paint/anodize to prevent corrosion. Inspect for cracks/dents. Avoid water traps.
key_facts: High strength-to-weight ratio
Susceptible to corrosion (salt, moisture)
Paint and anodizing prevent corrosion
Stress-corrosion cracking at corrosion pits
Regular inspection for cracks/wrinkles
Dents reduce fatigue strength
Avoid galvanic corrosion (dissimilar metals)
Proper drainage and ventilation essential
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.70.6(a)

syllabus_desc: Describe precautions for structural integrity with composite construction.
concept: Composite materials (carbon fiber, fiberglass, aramid) offer excellent strength-to-weight ratios, corrosion resistance, and design flexibility. Precautions for composite structural integrity include: (1) prevent water absorption (causes swelling and delamination); (2) avoid UV damage (degrades resin matrix); (3) protect from impact damage (composites are less forgiving of dents than metal; barely-visible impact damage can reduce strength significantly); (4) prevent moisture ingress through cracks or voids; (5) maintain sealants and coatings to prevent water entry; (6) avoid excessive heat (resin matrix degrades above certain temperatures); (7) use only authorized repairs by trained technicians (improper repairs are catastrophic). Composite damage is often invisible to external inspection.
exam_tip: Composites avoid corrosion but need UV/water/impact protection. Invisible damage is critical. Authorized repairs only.
key_facts: High strength-to-weight ratio
Excellent corrosion resistance
Design flexibility
Susceptible to water absorption/delamination
UV damage degrades resin
Impact damage often invisible
BVID (barely-visible impact damage) critical
Proper sealing and coatings essential
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.70.6(b)

syllabus_desc: Describe precautions for structural integrity with fabric-covered construction.
concept: Fabric-covered aircraft (fabric over wooden or metal framework) require careful maintenance for structural integrity. Precautions include: (1) protect fabric from moisture (causes rot of wooden framework and loss of strength); (2) maintain protective dope or acrylic coating (prevents UV damage and water entry); (3) inspect regularly for tears, holes, or loose areas (compromises aerodynamic surface and allows water to enter); (4) protect from ultraviolet radiation (degrades fabric and dope); (5) allow proper ventilation (prevents moisture accumulation); (6) avoid extreme temperature fluctuations (causes fabric shrinkage/expansion and coating cracking); (7) use proper repairs by qualified personnel (a torn wing cover is a critical airworthiness issue). Fabric can harbor hidden damage (internal rot, corrosion) not visible externally.
exam_tip: Fabric needs water and UV protection. Dope coating prevents damage. Inspect for tears. Wooden framework rots if wet.
key_facts: Fabric requires protective dope/coating
Water causes wooden frame rot
UV degrades fabric and dope
Regular inspection for tears/damage
Maintain protective envelope integrity
Proper ventilation prevents moisture
Extreme temperature causes cracking
Internal rot may not be visible
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.70.6(c)

syllabus_desc: Describe indications of damage or failure in aluminium structures.
concept: Indications of damage or failure in aluminium include: (1) visible cracks (especially at corners, fastener holes, or where rivets are missing); (2) dents or deformation (indicate overstress, impact, or corrosion); (3) white or grey corrosion (aluminum oxide, indicates active corrosion); (4) green or white deposits (galvanic corrosion near dissimilar metals); (5) wrinkles or buckles (indicate compression failure or fatigue); (6) fastener pull-through or fastener holes enlarged (loss of structure); (7) gaps in joints or separated panels; (8) stress marks or paint cracking (indicate material stress). Regular visual inspection can detect these, but crack detection may require non-destructive testing (NDT).
exam_tip: Look for: cracks, dents, corrosion (white/green), wrinkles, pulled fasteners, gaps. Paint cracking indicates stress.
key_facts: Visible cracks at corners or fasteners
Dents or deformation
White/grey corrosion (oxidation)
Green deposits (galvanic corrosion)
Wrinkles or buckling
Fastener pull-through
Enlarged fastener holes
Separated panels or joints
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.70.8(a)

syllabus_desc: Describe indications of damage or failure in composite structures.
concept: Indications of damage or failure in composite structures include: (1) delamination (layers separating internally; tapping reveals hollow sound); (2) fiber waviness or bunching (improper manufacture or impact); (3) surface cracks in the resin matrix (may indicate internal damage); (4) crazing (fine surface cracks, often from water absorption); (5) discoloration or bleaching (UV damage or water damage); (6) soft areas or lack of rigidity (loss of fiber-resin bond); (7) wrinkles in the skin (impact damage or manufacturing defect); (8) visible holes or tears. Barely-visible impact damage (BVID) is critical: composites can be severely weakened by impacts that leave minimal visible marks. Tap testing and ultrasonic inspection can detect delamination.
exam_tip: Composites: delamination (hollow tap), wrinkles, crazing, discoloration. Barely-visible damage is critical. Tap test used.
key_facts: Delamination (layers separating)
Hollow sound on tap test
Fiber waviness
Surface cracks in resin
Crazing (fine surface cracks)
Discoloration or bleaching
Loss of rigidity
Barely-visible impact damage
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.70.8(b)

syllabus_desc: Describe indications of damage or failure in fabric-covered structures.
concept: Indications of damage or failure in fabric-covered structures include: (1) tears or holes in fabric (allows water and air to enter); (2) loose or sagging fabric (loss of aerodynamic shape and structural efficiency); (3) peeling or flaking dope/coating (exposes fabric to UV and water); (4) water stains or discoloration (indicates water damage or seepage); (5) soft or spongy areas in wooden framework (indicates rot or decay); (6) delamination of wooden spars or ribs (wood rot from moisture); (7) deteriorated glue joints (strength loss); (8) pinhole or small perforations (from insects or corrosion). Fabric damage is often visible and rapidly progressive. Water damage to wooden structures is catastrophic and may not be immediately apparent (internal rot).
exam_tip: Fabric: tears, loose areas, peeling dope, water stains, soft spots, glue failures. Water rot is hidden and catastrophic.
key_facts: Tears or holes in fabric
Loose or sagging fabric
Peeling or flaking dope
Water stains or discoloration
Soft/spongy wooden areas (rot)
Delaminated wooden structures
Failed glue joints
Pinhole perforations
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.70.8(c)

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedure to tie down (or picket) a light aircraft.
concept: Tie-down procedure: (1) Position aircraft into wind with brakes set. (2) Attach three tie-down ropes/cables: nose ring/tie-down point (forward), wing lift strut or hard point (both sides, aft). (3) Route cables/ropes to ground anchors at angles approximately 45-60° (avoid vertical pulling). (4) Secure nose tie-down taut but not excessive tension (allow slight give for wind movement). (5) Cross-tie wings: use diagonal straps or ropes from one wing to opposite side anchor, securing over fuselage or cockpit area. (6) Install pitot tube cover and control locks if available. (7) Check wind forecast; if strong winds expected, use additional tie-downs. (8) Remove tie-downs before flight by reversing sequence. Proper tie-down prevents aircraft drift, wind damage, and propeller contact during strong winds.
exam_tip: Three tie-downs: nose + wings at 45-60° angles to ground.
key_facts: Wind-facing position
Three main tie-down points
Ground angle 45-60°
Wing cross-tie diagonal
Control locks if equipped
Pitot cover protection
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.70.10

syllabus_desc: Describe blade section in propeller terminology.
concept: A propeller blade section (or blade element) is a small spanwise segment of the blade at a particular distance from the hub. Each section of the propeller blade has its own aerodynamic properties and operates at a different angle of attack because the blade twists along its length. The cross-sectional shape (profile) of each section along the blade span is typically an aerofoil shape, similar to a wing section but optimized for propeller duty. Different sections along the blade span have different chord lengths (chord decreases toward the tip) and different profiles. The blade section concept is used to analyze propeller performance by calculating forces on each section and integrating across the blade span.
exam_tip: Blade section is a slice along the blade span. Each section is an aerofoil. Angle of attack varies along span.
key_facts: Cross-sectional slice along blade span
Aerofoil-shaped profile
Chord varies along span (smaller at tip)
Each section has different angle of attack
Profile optimized for propeller duty
Blade twist varies along span
Used for performance analysis
Different sections have different forces
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.72.2(a)

syllabus_desc: Describe blade angle in propeller terminology.
concept: Blade angle (also called pitch angle or blade pitch) is the angle between the blade chord (reference line across the aerofoil) and the plane of rotation (perpendicular to the propeller axis). It is typically measured at a specified radius from the hub (e.g., 0.75 blade radius). Blade angle determines the angle of attack at which the blade operates. A larger blade angle results in a steeper pitch and higher angle of attack. Variable-pitch propellers allow blade angle to be changed in flight to optimize performance for different flight phases (high blade angle for cruise, low for climb). Fixed-pitch propellers have a blade angle set by design and cannot be changed. Blade angle is measured in degrees and is a key design parameter for propeller efficiency.
exam_tip: Blade angle = chord angle relative to plane of rotation. Larger angle = steeper pitch. Variable-pitch props change angle in flight.
key_facts: Angle between blade chord and rotation plane
Measured at reference radius (e.g., 0.75 R)
Determines blade angle of attack
Measured in degrees from rotation plane
Fixed-pitch prop: angle is fixed
Variable-pitch prop: angle adjustable
Larger angle = steeper pitch, higher AOA
Key design and performance parameter
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.72.2(b)

syllabus_desc: Describe helix angle or pitch angle in propeller geometry.
concept: The helix angle (also called pitch angle or helix pitch) describes the geometric path a propeller blade traces through the air as it rotates. If a propeller blade were to cut through the air without rotating, it would follow a straight axial path; but because the blade rotates, it traces a helical (spiral) path. The helix angle is the angle between the blade chord and the relative wind (or equivalently, the angle between the plane of rotation and the path traced by the blade through space). The helix angle increases along the blade span as airspeed increases (at the tip, higher airspeed results in a steeper helix angle than at the root). This is related to the concept of blade twist: the blade is twisted so that the blade angle (pitch) is larger at the root (where the propeller moves slower) and smaller at the tip (where airspeed is higher due to blade rotation speed).
exam_tip: Helix angle is the angle of blade path through air. Blade rotates and advances axially, creating spiral path. Helix angle varies along span.
key_facts: Angle of blade path through air
Results from rotation + axial advance
Spiraling (helical) path through air
Increases along blade span
Increases with airspeed
Related to blade twist
Root has lower helix angle than tip
Critical for pitch concept understanding
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.72.2(c)

syllabus_desc: Describe angle of attack in propeller blade sections.
concept: The angle of attack of a propeller blade section is the angle between the blade chord and the relative wind experienced by that section. The relative wind for a propeller blade is the vector sum of the forward airspeed (V) and the rotational velocity of that blade section (ωR, where ω is rotation rate in rad/s and R is distance from hub). Different sections along the blade have different rotational velocities and experience different relative winds. At the root, most of the relative wind comes from rotation; at the tip, the relative wind is dominated by forward airspeed at cruise. The blade is twisted so that the angle of attack is relatively constant along the blade span for efficient operation. Angle of attack varies with airspeed and RPM.
exam_tip: Propeller AOA = angle between chord and relative wind. Relative wind = vector sum of airspeed + rotational velocity.
key_facts: Angle between blade chord and relative wind
Relative wind = V (forward) + ωR (rotational)
Different at each blade station
Root: relative wind mostly from rotation
Tip: relative wind mostly from airspeed
Blade twist keeps AOA relatively constant
AOA varies with airspeed and RPM
Efficient operation requires proper AOA
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.72.2(d)

syllabus_desc: Explain the reason for blade (or helical) twist.
concept: Propeller blade twists from root to tip (higher pitch angle at root, lower at tip) because velocity changes along blade radius. Tip moves much faster than root (V = radius × RPM). At high tip speed but low aircraft airspeed, blade tip experiences low angle of attack. Root experiences higher relative velocity from aircraft motion, higher angle of attack. Twist compensates for this variation: root blade angle lower (relative to rotation plane) to increase angle of attack at tip, and higher at root. Optimal twist maintains relatively constant angle of attack along blade, ensuring efficient thrust generation along entire blade length. Without twist, outer blade stalls while root operates below optimal efficiency.
exam_tip: Twist compensates for speed variation along blade.
key_facts: Root vs tip velocity difference
Angle of attack variation
Efficient twist gradient
Stall avoidance along blade
Thrust optimization
Blade efficiency maintenance
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.72.4

syllabus_desc: Describe direction of rotation of a propeller blade section.
concept: The direction of rotation of a propeller blade section is the direction in which that section moves through space as the propeller rotates. For a clockwise-rotating propeller (viewed from behind/front), each blade section rotates in a circular path around the propeller hub. The direction of rotation is either clockwise (CW) or counterclockwise (CCW) as viewed from the direction the propeller pulls (from behind the engine toward the tail). The rotation direction is important for determining the direction of the relative wind experienced by the blade section. As the blade rotates, each section moves through the air, and the resulting relative wind is the vector sum of the rotational velocity and the forward airspeed.
exam_tip: Direction of rotation: CW or CCW (viewed from direction of pull). Rotation direction affects relative wind direction.
key_facts: Circular motion around hub
Clockwise (CW) or counterclockwise (CCW)
Viewed from direction of pull
Each section moves in circular path
Determines relative wind direction
Related to gyroscopic effects
Design and aerodynamic parameter
Different at root and tip but same direction
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.72.6(a)

syllabus_desc: Describe relative airflow experienced by a rotating blade section.
concept: The relative airflow experienced by a propeller blade section is the vector sum of two velocity components: (1) forward airspeed (V, parallel to propeller axis), and (2) rotational velocity of that blade section (ωR, tangent to the circular path). The resultant relative wind direction and magnitude depend on both components. At the root of the blade, rotational velocity is low, so forward airspeed dominates the relative wind direction, which is nearly axial (parallel to the propeller axis). At the tip, rotational velocity is high (proportional to radius), so the relative wind has a larger tangential component and is more perpendicular to the blade radius. The relative wind angle changes along the blade span, which is why the blade must be twisted (higher blade angle at the root, lower at the tip) to maintain a relatively constant angle of attack.
exam_tip: Relative airflow = V (forward) + ωR (rotational velocity). Root wind nearly axial; tip wind more tangential.
key_facts: Vector sum of forward and rotational velocity
Root: primarily forward airspeed
Tip: significant rotational velocity component
Relative wind direction varies along span
Requires blade twist for constant AOA
Magnitude increases with airspeed and RPM
Direction perpendicular to blade chord ideally
Critical for understanding blade forces
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.72.6(b)

syllabus_desc: Describe total reaction and its components (lift and drag) for a rotating blade section.
concept: The total reaction is the net aerodynamic force acting on a propeller blade section. Like a wing, the blade section experiences lift (perpendicular to relative wind) and drag (parallel to relative wind, opposing motion). The total reaction is the vector sum of lift and drag. In propeller terminology, the total reaction is often decomposed into thrust and torque (or torque reaction). Thrust is the component of total reaction along the propeller axis (useful, pulling force). Torque is the component perpendicular to the axis (rotational resistance that the engine must overcome). The magnitude of total reaction depends on the relative wind speed, blade area, blade shape (Cₗ and Cₓ), and angle of attack. As airspeed increases, the relative wind speed decreases (for fixed RPM), which reduces total reaction magnitude and changes its direction.
exam_tip: Total reaction = aerodynamic force from lift+drag. Decomposes into thrust (axial) and torque (rotational).
key_facts: Aerodynamic force on blade section
Vector sum of lift and drag
Lift perpendicular to relative wind
Drag parallel to relative wind
Thrust component along axis (useful)
Torque component perpendicular (resistance)
Magnitude depends on relative wind speed
Direction changes with airspeed/RPM
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.72.6(c)

syllabus_desc: Describe thrust and propeller torque for a rotating blade section.
concept: Thrust is the useful axial force produced by the propeller, pushing the aircraft forward. It is the component of total reaction along the propeller axis. Thrust results from the lift on the blade section, with drag reducing the effective thrust. Higher blade angle and higher relative wind speed increase thrust (up to a limit before aerodynamic stall). Propeller torque (or torque reaction) is the rotational resistance that opposes the rotation of the propeller and must be overcome by the engine. It results from the drag on the blade sections. Torque is the couple (moment) that the engine must provide to maintain propeller rotation. As airspeed increases (at fixed RPM), the relative wind decreases, so both thrust and torque may decrease (the propeller becomes less loaded). Variable-pitch propellers adjust blade angle to maintain constant propeller loading and thus constant power absorption across the speed range.
exam_tip: Thrust = forward component of total reaction (useful pull). Torque = rotational resistance (drag-based).
key_facts: Thrust is axial propeller force
Pushes aircraft forward
Component of total reaction
Increases with blade angle (to limit)
Decreases with increasing airspeed
Propeller torque is rotational resistance
Engine must overcome torque
Torque increases with power demand
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.72.6(d)

syllabus_desc: For a fixed-pitch propeller at a constant throttle setting, explain the relationship between airspeed, angle of attack and rpm.
concept: Fixed-pitch propeller at constant throttle (constant RPM): as airspeed increases, relative airflow changes angle, reducing blade angle of attack (aircraft motion reduces relative angle between airflow and blade section). Decreased angle of attack reduces thrust slightly and drag on blade. With constant RPM and increased speed, relative pitch angle decreases. As airspeed decreases: blade angle of attack increases, thrust and drag on blade increase, propeller becomes less efficient (absorbs more engine power). Aircraft speed naturally stabilizes where power required equals power available at set throttle. Fixed-pitch propeller optimization for one speed; less efficient at other speeds.
exam_tip: Fixed-pitch: increasing speed decreases blade angle of attack.
key_facts: Constant RPM at throttle setting
Airspeed affects relative wind
Angle of attack change
Thrust variation with speed
Power absorption change
Efficiency dependency
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.72.8

syllabus_desc: Briefly explain the factors which affect the ability of a fixed-pitch propeller to convert engine power into useful thrust.
concept: Factors affecting fixed-pitch propeller efficiency: (1) Blade design: pitch angle, twist, airfoil shape determine angle of attack at various speeds. (2) Airspeed: optimal efficiency only at design speed; inefficient at other speeds. (3) RPM: higher RPM increases blade velocity, changes angle of attack. (4) Aircraft weight: heavier aircraft requires more thrust, absorbs more engine power. (5) Propeller diameter: larger diameter more efficient (moves more air at lower velocity). (6) Density altitude: lower air density reduces thrust available and propeller efficiency. (7) Engine power: limited by fuel/air mixture and ignition timing. Slip: difference between geometric pitch and actual distance traveled (thrust loss). Fixed-pitch propeller compromise design; constant-speed propeller maintains optimal blade angle across speed range.
exam_tip: Efficiency optimal at design speed; poorest at high altitude.
key_facts: Blade design efficiency
Airspeed mismatch loss
RPM optimization
Weight loading effect
Diameter thrust relationship
Density altitude impact
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.72.10

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic principle of operation for a constant-speed propeller, and the normal procedure for changing power settings with the manifold pressure and pitch controls.
concept: Constant-speed (variable-pitch) propeller: hydraulic mechanism automatically adjusts blade angle to maintain constant RPM. Governor senses RPM; if RPM drops below set value, governor allows oil pressure to decrease, centrifugal weights pitch blades coarser (higher angle), reducing drag on blades, increasing RPM. If RPM exceeds set value, governor increases oil pressure, pitch becomes finer (lower angle), increasing drag, decreasing RPM. Procedure for power changes: (1) Set desired RPM first with propeller control (fine pitch for high RPM, coarse for low RPM). (2) Adjust manifold pressure (throttle) to desired power setting. (3) Monitor engine parameters; propeller governor maintains RPM automatically as pitch changes. (4) During descent, reduce RPM first, then reduce power. This sequence avoids over-boost and roughness.
exam_tip: Constant-speed propeller maintains set RPM automatically.
key_facts: Governor mechanism operation
Centrifugal weight response
Oil pressure control
RPM feedback
Pitch angle variation
Coarse vs fine blade angle
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.72.12

syllabus_desc: State the principal advantage of a constant-speed versus a fixed-pitch propeller.
concept: Principal advantages of constant-speed propeller: (1) Optimal blade angle maintained across entire speed range, maximizing efficiency at all speeds and power settings. (2) Better climb performance: at takeoff, propeller automatically pitches fine for quick acceleration and climb; at cruise, automatically adjusts to coarser pitch for economical speed. (3) Better performance on high-altitude airfields: constant-speed propeller more responsive to density altitude changes. (4) Flexibility: pilot can optimize performance by setting RPM for climb (high RPM, fine pitch) or cruise (lower RPM, coarse pitch). Fixed-pitch propeller compromise design, efficient only at one design speed; constant-speed superior performance across flight envelope. Disadvantage: higher cost and maintenance complexity.
exam_tip: Constant-speed maintains optimal efficiency across speed range.
key_facts: Efficiency across speeds
Automatic blade angle optimization
Climb performance improvement
Altitude performance benefit
Pilot flexibility
Cost-complexity tradeoff
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.72.14

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and operation of a typical reduction gearbox.
concept: Reduction gearbox (propeller reduction gearbox): reduces propeller RPM while increasing torque, allowing engine to run at higher RPM (more efficient) while propeller operates at lower optimal RPM. Typical ratio 2:1 or 3:1 (engine 3000 RPM = propeller 1000 RPM at 3:1 ratio). Function: (1) Mechanical advantage for torque (torque multiplied by gear ratio). (2) Allows smaller diameter propeller at high RPM equivalent to larger diameter at low RPM. (3) Reduces propeller noise (lower RPM). Components: input shaft from engine, gear sets (spur or epicyclic/planetary), output shaft to propeller. Operation: engine power drives input gear(s), which drive output gear(s) at reduced speed/increased torque. Some systems include automatic clutch or sprag clutch for propeller shutdown (engine stops, propeller free-wheels).
exam_tip: Reduction gearbox multiplies torque, reduces RPM.
key_facts: Gear ratio typical 2:1 to 3:1
Torque multiplication
Engine efficiency optimization
Propeller speed reduction
Noise reduction benefit
Epicyclic gear design
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.72.16

syllabus_desc: Describe common methods of mechanical operation of primary flight controls.
concept: Primary flight controls (elevators, ailerons, rudder) are typically operated by mechanical cable or rod linkages between the control column/wheel and the control surface hinges. In cable-operated systems, pulling the control column back pulls cables connected to the elevator horn, deflecting the elevator up. Side stick or yoke movements rotate the ailerons via cables connected to cable drums. The rudder is operated by foot pedals connected via cables to the rudder horn. Mechanical linkages include pulleys, forks, quadrants, and torque tubes. Many modern aircraft use a mix of mechanical and hydraulic systems: mechanical linkage from controls to a hydraulic valve, then hydraulic pressure to actuate the control surface. Mechanical advantage (leverage ratios) is designed into the linkages to reduce pilot force requirements while maintaining control sensitivity.
exam_tip: Primary controls: cables/rods from column to control surfaces. Mechanical advantage via pulleys and quadrants.
key_facts: Cable or rod linkage systems
Control column to elevator via cables
Yoke to ailerons via cable drums
Foot pedals to rudder via cables
Pulleys and quadrants provide mechanical advantage
Hydraulic actuation in modern aircraft
Mechanical advantage reduces pilot force
Maintains control sensitivity
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.76.2(a)

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of trim tabs in aircraft control systems.
concept: Trim tabs are small control surfaces mounted on the trailing edges of primary control surfaces (elevator, ailerons, rudder). They serve to reduce pilot workload by maintaining the aircraft in equilibrium without continuous control inputs. An elevator trim tab is a small surface on the elevator trailing edge; moving the trim control (wheel or lever) adjusts the tab angle, which changes the elevator deflection indirectly by creating aerodynamic forces. The pilot moves the trim control to adjust the trim tab, which generates a moment that deflects the primary control surface to a new equilibrium position, reducing the need for sustained control column pressure. Trim can be mechanical (cable-operated tab adjustment) or electric (motor-driven). Proper trim setup is essential for comfortable, efficient flight and for fuel efficiency during cruise.
exam_tip: Trim tabs reduce pilot workload. Tab creates moment that deflects primary surface. Pilot adjusts trim control (wheel/lever).
key_facts: Small surfaces on primary control trailing edges
Reduce pilot workload
Maintain aircraft in equilibrium
Generate aerodynamic moments
Mechanical or electric operation
Elevator, aileron, rudder trim available
Essential for cruise efficiency
Requires different technique per phase
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.76.2(b)

syllabus_desc: Describe common methods of mechanical operation of flap systems.
concept: Flap systems deploy flaps (control surfaces on the wing trailing edge) to increase wing camber and area, improving low-speed handling and reducing landing distance. Mechanical flap operation typically uses a hand crank or lever in the cockpit connected via cable and pulley systems to flap hinges. The cable-driven system pulls the flaps down and back (down-and-back motion), and a mechanical linkage ensures both flaps deflect symmetrically. Some aircraft use electrically-powered flap actuation with a motor driving a screw or gear mechanism, while the pilot selects flap position from a switch. Hydraulic systems use pressure to actuate flap cylinders. Mechanical flap systems typically have detents or positions: 0° (up), 10°, 20°, 30° (full flap), allowing partial flap deployment for approach and full deployment for landing. Over-speed protection is essential; flap deployment at high speed can cause structural damage.
exam_tip: Flaps use cable, electric, or hydraulic systems. Hand crank or switch selects position. Common positions: 0°, 10°, 20°, 30°.
key_facts: Mechanical cable systems (hand crank)
Electric motor-driven systems
Hydraulic actuators
Down-and-back motion
Symmetrical deployment via linkage
Flap positions: 0°, 10°, 20°, 30°
Speed limits for deployment
Essential for approach and landing
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.76.2(c)

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of control locks and precautions for removal before flight.
concept: Control locks: mechanical devices preventing flight control movement while aircraft parked, protecting aircraft from wind damage and preventing accidental control deflection. Locations: (1) Elevator lock: secures control column, prevents up/down movement. (2) Aileron locks: typically cross-cabin, prevent roll movement. (3) Rudder lock: prevents pedal movement. Precautions for removal: (1) Remove all locks before entering cockpit or VERY FIRST preflight action. (2) Check control free and correct movement throughout range (full up/down for elevator, full left/right for ailerons, full left/right for rudder). (3) Verify trim controls move freely. (4) Install control locks only after flight operations complete, immediately before leaving aircraft. Forgetting to remove locks catastrophic; attempting flight with locked controls results in total loss of control authority.
exam_tip: Remove control locks first preflight; reinstall last post-flight.
key_facts: Elevator lock function
Aileron lock function
Rudder lock function
Wind protection purpose
Free and correct movement check
Flight impossibility if locked
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.76.4

syllabus_desc: Describe the normal operational use of flaps, including limitations.
concept: Flap use: (1) Takeoff: 0-15° (10-15° provides good lift increase with manageable drag increase). (2) Climb: retract to 0° or maintain small deflection (10°) depending on obstacle clearance requirement. (3) Cruise: retract fully (0°) for speed/efficiency. (4) Approach: extend progressively as speed decreases (10° initial approach, 25° base leg, 40° final approach). (5) Landing: full extension (40°) for maximum drag and shortest landing distance. Limitations: (1) Speed limitations: flaps beyond 10° typically limited to 110-120 knots; beyond 25° limited to 70-90 knots; full flaps limited to 50-65 knots (check POH). (2) Altitude: not recommended above typical 3000 feet (check aircraft limitations). (3) Asymmetric flap extension: if flaps extend unequally, creates aileron trim requirement; if severe, discontinue operation and land with flaps extended symmetrically. (4) Progressive extension: extend flaps gradually (may have intermediate positions).
exam_tip: Takeoff 10-15°, landing 40°; follow POH speed limits.
key_facts: Progressive extension technique
Speed limitation envelope
Altitude limitations
Asymmetric flap recognition
Drag increase benefits
Lift increase benefits
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.76.6

syllabus_desc: Describe the general effects of altitude on aircraft performance
concept: As altitude increases, air density decreases. This reduces engine power output (less oxygen for combustion) and reduces aerodynamic performance (less lift per unit area at the same TAS). The result is reduced rate of climb, longer takeoff and landing distances, and reduced maximum speed. The aircraft must fly at a higher TAS to produce the same lift, because the reduced air density means the dynamic pressure (½ρV²) is lower at any given TAS.
exam_tip: Higher altitude = less dense air = less power + more TAS needed for same lift = degraded performance all round.
key_facts: Increasing altitude reduces air density
Reduced density decreases engine power output
Higher TAS needed for the same IAS/lift
Rate of climb decreases with altitude
Takeoff and landing distances increase
Service ceiling: altitude where rate of climb = 100 ft/min
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6 (performance), AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.100.2

syllabus_desc: Define pressure altitude
concept: Pressure altitude is the altitude indicated on the altimeter when the subscale is set to the standard datum of 1013.25 hPa (29.92 inHg). It represents the height in the International Standard Atmosphere (ISA) corresponding to the prevailing atmospheric pressure. Pressure altitude is used in performance calculations because aircraft performance depends on air density, which correlates with pressure altitude rather than true altitude.
exam_tip: Pressure altitude = altimeter reading with 1013.25 hPa set. It's the altitude in ISA that matches your actual air pressure.
key_facts: Set altimeter subscale to 1013.25 hPa
Read the altimeter — that is pressure altitude
Used for all performance calculations
Does not represent true height above sea level
Standard datum: 1013.25 hPa / 29.92 inHg
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (ISA), Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.100.4

syllabus_desc: Calculate aerodrome pressure altitude, given aerodrome elevation and prevailing QNH
concept: Pressure altitude = Aerodrome Elevation + (1013 − QNH) × 30. For every 1 hPa below 1013, add 30 ft to the elevation; for every 1 hPa above 1013, subtract 30 ft. Example: aerodrome elevation 500 ft, QNH 1003 hPa → pressure altitude = 500 + (1013 − 1003) × 30 = 500 + 300 = 800 ft.
exam_tip: Formula: PA = Elevation + (1013 − QNH) × 30. Below standard pressure = higher pressure altitude.
key_facts: PA = Elevation + (1013 − QNH) × 30
1 hPa difference ≈ 30 ft change in altitude
QNH below 1013 → PA higher than elevation
QNH above 1013 → PA lower than elevation
Used to enter performance charts
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3, Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.100.6

syllabus_desc: Explain how to determine pressure altitude by using an altimeter
concept: Set the altimeter subscale to 1013.25 hPa (the ISA standard datum). The altitude shown on the altimeter is the pressure altitude. This is the quickest method in the cockpit — simply dial 1013 into the subscale window and read the altimeter directly. On the ground at an aerodrome, this gives the aerodrome pressure altitude for entering performance charts.
exam_tip: Easiest method: set 1013 on the subscale, read the altimeter. That's your pressure altitude.
key_facts: Set subscale to 1013.25 hPa
Read the altimeter display directly
Result is pressure altitude
Works both on the ground and in flight
Quick cockpit method for performance chart entry
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (instruments), AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.100.8

syllabus_desc: Explain the general effect of temperature on performance
concept: Higher temperatures reduce air density, which degrades aircraft performance. In a hot atmosphere, the same pressure altitude has lower air density than in ISA conditions — the engine produces less power (less oxygen per unit volume), the propeller is less efficient, and more TAS is needed for the same lift. The result is longer takeoff rolls, reduced rate of climb, and longer landing distances. Conversely, cold temperatures increase density and improve performance.
exam_tip: Hot = high = horrible for performance. Cold = better density = better performance.
key_facts: Higher temperature → lower air density
Lower density → less engine power (less O₂)
Longer takeoff and landing distances
Reduced rate of climb
Temperature above ISA = worse than standard performance
Temperature below ISA = better than standard performance
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3, Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.100.10

syllabus_desc: Define density altitude
concept: Density altitude is pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature. It represents the altitude in the ISA at which the prevailing air density would be found. Density altitude is the single best indicator of aircraft performance — when density altitude is high, performance is degraded. A simple approximation: Density Altitude ≈ Pressure Altitude + (120 × ISA deviation in °C). If the temperature is above ISA, density altitude is higher than pressure altitude.
exam_tip: Density altitude = pressure altitude corrected for temperature. High density altitude = poor performance.
key_facts: DA = PA + (120 × ISA deviation)
ISA deviation = actual temp − ISA temp at that PA
ISA temp at sea level = 15°C, lapse rate 2°C/1000 ft
High DA = poor performance (hot, high, humid)
DA is the best single indicator of aircraft performance
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3, Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.100.12

syllabus_desc: ISA deviation and its calculation and effect
concept: ISA (International Standard Atmosphere) deviation is calculated as the difference between actual temperature and standard temperature at a given altitude. The relationship: ISA deviation (°C) = actual temperature − standard temperature at that altitude.
exam_tip: Remember ISA temp at sea level is 15°C and decreases by 2°C per 1000 ft. If actual temp is 25°C at sea level, ISA deviation is +10°C. A positive ISA deviation (warmer than standard) increases density altitude.
key_facts: ISA standard temperature at sea level = 15°C
ISA lapse rate = 2°C per 1000 ft (or 6.5°C per 1000 m)
ISA deviation = actual temperature − standard temperature
Positive ISA deviation = warmer than standard = higher density altitude
Negative ISA deviation = cooler than standard = lower density altitude
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: Density altitude: given pressure altitude and ambient temperature, calculate density altitude
concept: Density altitude is the altitude in the standard atmosphere at which the air density would be equivalent to the actual air density at the aircraft's operating location. It is calculated using pressure altitude and temperature; higher density altitude indicates thinner air and reduced aircraft performance.
exam_tip: Use the density altitude formula or chart: DA = PA + (120 × ISA deviation). For example, if pressure altitude is 5000 ft and temperature is 25°C (ISA dev = +5°C), then DA = 5000 + (120 × 5) = 5600 ft. Higher DA means worse performance.
key_facts: Density Altitude (ft) = Pressure Altitude + (120 × ISA deviation in °C)
Pressure altitude is read from altimeter when subscale is set to 29.92 inHg
Density altitude directly affects aircraft takeoff distance, rate of climb, and landing distance
Hot, high-altitude airfields produce very high density altitudes
At sea level in standard conditions, density altitude = 0 ft
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: Effect of aircraft weight on take-off and landing distances
concept: Aircraft weight affects both takeoff distance required (TODR) and landing distance required (LDR). Greater weight increases both distances because more energy is needed for acceleration and deceleration. The relationship is non-linear; doubling weight approximately doubles or increases distance more than proportionally.
exam_tip: Heavier aircraft have longer TODR and LDR. Weight affects both the acceleration phase (heavier = slower acceleration) and the distance needed to reach flying speed. Always check maximum authorized weight limits and ensure TODR/LDR data corresponds to actual loaded weight.
key_facts: Increased weight increases both TODR and LDR
Weight increases required acceleration distance (more inertia to overcome)
Weight increases distance needed to achieve flying speed
Landing distance increases with weight due to increased inertia and landing speed
Performance charts account for specific weight configurations
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: Effect of temperature and pressure (density altitude) on take-off and landing distances
concept: Temperature and pressure determine density altitude; higher density altitude (thinner air) increases TODR and LDR. Hot, high-altitude conditions create the worst performance scenario. The effect is proportional to the density altitude value relative to pressure altitude.
exam_tip: High density altitude = poor performance. Remember: a high, hot day is the worst combination. Performance decreases by approximately 10-20% per 1000 ft of density altitude increase above actual pressure altitude. Always consult performance charts for the actual DA, not just altitude.
key_facts: High density altitude increases TODR and LDR significantly
Density altitude accounts for both pressure and temperature effects simultaneously
Performance degrades as air becomes thinner (higher DA)
Performance charts are indexed by density altitude, not pressure altitude
Temperature effect on performance is greater at higher altitudes
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: Effect of humidity on take-off and landing distances
concept: Humidity affects air density; moist air is slightly less dense than dry air at the same temperature and pressure. High humidity slightly increases density altitude and thus increases TODR and LDR, though the effect is small compared to temperature and pressure. Very dry conditions slightly improve performance.
exam_tip: Humidity's effect on performance is small and often neglected in basic performance calculations, but exam questions may mention it. High humidity slightly worsens performance. This effect is built into some advanced performance charts but may be ignored for basic calculations.
key_facts: High humidity decreases air density slightly (moist air is lighter)
High humidity increases density altitude slightly
Effect of humidity is much smaller than effects of temperature or pressure
Humidity is rarely the primary limiting factor for takeoff or landing
Very dry conditions provide marginally better performance
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: Effect of runway slope on take-off and landing distances
concept: Upslope decreases TODR (gravity assists takeoff climb) but increases LDR (gravity opposes landing deceleration). Downslope increases TODR (gravity opposes climb) but decreases LDR (gravity assists landing). Slope effects vary with runway inclination and are typically stated as degrees or percentage grade.
exam_tip: Up the hill = easier takeoff, harder landing. Down the hill = harder takeoff, easier landing. A 2% upslope can reduce TODR by approximately 10-15%; a 2% downslope can increase TODR by similar amounts. Always use runways oriented to minimize required distance.
key_facts: Upslope reduces TODR (assists climb with gravity component)
Upslope increases LDR (gravity opposes landing deceleration)
Downslope increases TODR (gravity opposes climb)
Downslope reduces LDR (gravity assists landing deceleration)
Effect magnitude depends on slope angle and aircraft type
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: Effect of runway surface condition on take-off and landing distances
concept: Runway surface condition affects friction between tyres and runway. Wet, contaminated, or unprepared surfaces reduce friction, increasing both TODR and LDR. Wet grass, snow, slush, or gravel all significantly degrade performance. Surface condition effects are sometimes quantified as drag coefficients or distance multipliers.
exam_tip: Wet surface = increased TODR and LDR. Grass airfields significantly increase required distances. Contaminated surfaces (mud, gravel, snow) can double or triple required distance. Always inspect runway conditions before flight and assess braking action reports.
key_facts: Poor surface conditions increase friction drag coefficient
Wet surface increases both TODR and LDR
Grass airfields increase required distance (typically 25-50% increase)
Gravel, snow, or slush can dramatically increase required distance
Surface contamination reduces tyre grip and increases stopping distance
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: Effect of headwind and tailwind components on take-off and landing distances
concept: Headwind reduces TODR (provides additional airspeed for takeoff) and reduces LDR (provides higher approach airspeed, aiding deceleration). Tailwind increases both TODR and LDR (reduces available airspeed for takeoff, reduces deceleration effectiveness). Wind component effect varies with runway heading and wind direction.
exam_tip: Headwind is beneficial for both takeoff and landing. Tailwind is detrimental. A 10-knot headwind can reduce TODR by approximately 15-20% depending on aircraft type. Maximum demonstrated tailwind for operations is typically very limited (often 5-10 knots). Always consider wind component when selecting runway.
key_facts: Headwind reduces TODR (increases effective airspeed for takeoff)
Headwind reduces LDR (increases approach airspeed for braking)
Tailwind increases TODR (reduces effective takeoff speed)
Tailwind increases LDR (reduces braking effectiveness)
Wind component calculated as headwind/tailwind along runway heading
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: Effect of use and misuse of flaps on take-off and landing distances
concept: Flap selection significantly affects TODR and LDR. Appropriate flap setting for takeoff reduces TODR (generates lift more efficiently at lower speeds). Increased flap angle for landing reduces LDR (generates more lift at lower speed, steeper descent angle). Incorrect flap use (too much flap at takeoff, too little at landing) significantly increases required distances.
exam_tip: For takeoff: use manufacturer-recommended flap setting (typically 5-15°) to minimize TODR. Too much flap increases drag without proportional lift benefit. For landing: use maximum recommended flap (typically 30-40°) to maximize drag and lift, minimizing LDR. Never operate beyond flap limits.
key_facts: Takeoff flap setting (typically 5-15°) reduces TODR compared to no flap
Landing flap setting (typically 30-40°) significantly reduces LDR
Excessive takeoff flap increases drag without corresponding benefit
Insufficient landing flap increases landing distance substantially
Flap misuse (asymmetric, partial deployment, or damage) degrades performance
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: Effect of power available on take-off and landing distances
concept: Available power directly determines the rate of acceleration during takeoff and sustained climb ability. Reduced power (due to density altitude, engine condition, or throttle limitation) increases TODR dramatically. Power reserve affects ability to clear obstacles. Landing distance is less affected by power availability but affected by go-around capability.
exam_tip: Low power = long TODR. Factors reducing power: high density altitude, engine limitations, contaminated fuel system, prop governor malfunction. Always check engine performance before takeoff. Power limits for takeoff/climb must be adhered to per aircraft limitations.
key_facts: Available power determines takeoff acceleration rate
Reduced power increases TODR exponentially
Density altitude reduces available power (thinner air = less engine thrust)
Engine condition, fuel system contamination, and carburetor ice reduce power
Power must be verified as adequate before committing to takeoff
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: Effect of frost, contaminants, or damage on lifting surfaces on take-off and landing distances
concept: Frost, ice, dirt, or damage on wings or control surfaces disrupts smooth airflow, significantly reducing lift and increasing drag. Even light frost or contamination degrades aerodynamic efficiency. Damaged surfaces (dents, wrinkles, torn fabric) increase drag coefficient substantially. These defects increase both TODR and LDR and reduce climb capability.
exam_tip: Never fly with frost, ice, or snow on lifting surfaces. Thoroughly inspect and clean wings before flight. Frost, even if thin and hard to see, can reduce climb performance by 30% or more. Any surface damage must be assessed for continued airworthiness. Airframe integrity directly affects performance.
key_facts: Frost or ice on lifting surfaces increases drag coefficient significantly
Frost or ice reduces maximum lift coefficient
Contaminated surfaces disrupt laminar airflow
Surface damage (dents, wrinkles, tears) increases parasite drag
Even light contamination can reduce climb performance by 10-30%
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: Describe the hazards of windshear in the initial climb-out path, and on the approach path
concept: Windshear — a sudden change in wind speed and/or direction — is hazardous during takeoff and approach because the aircraft is close to the ground at low speed with limited energy margin. On takeoff, encountering a decreasing headwind or increasing tailwind causes a sudden loss of airspeed and lift, which may result in the aircraft being unable to climb or even descending into terrain. On approach, encountering windshear can cause sudden changes in airspeed and flight path — the aircraft may drop below the approach path. Microburst-induced windshear is particularly dangerous: the pilot first encounters a headwind (performance increase), then a strong downdraft and tailwind (severe performance decrease), often at the worst possible moment. Recovery requires immediate application of full power and, if necessary, go-around.
exam_tip: Windshear on takeoff/approach is extremely dangerous — you're low, slow, and have no margin. A tailwind shear causes sudden airspeed loss. Full power and go-around if in doubt.
key_facts: Windshear = sudden change in wind speed/direction
Most dangerous during takeoff and approach (low altitude, low speed)
Decreasing headwind → sudden airspeed loss → loss of lift
Microbursts: headwind → downdraft → tailwind sequence
Recovery: full power, maintain attitude, go-around
Limited energy margin near the ground makes recovery difficult
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.100.18

syllabus_desc: Given typical performance data, demonstrate the ability to determine TODR
concept: Using the aircraft's Pilot Operating Handbook (POH) or flight manual performance charts, determine the Take Off Distance Required (TODR). The process typically involves: (1) determine pressure altitude and temperature; (2) enter the chart with these values; (3) apply corrections for aircraft weight, headwind/tailwind component, runway slope, and surface condition. The chart may be a table, a graph, or a series of grids. Always use the most conservative figures — round up pressure altitude and temperature, round down headwind. The result is the ground roll plus the distance to clear a 50 ft screen height obstacle.
exam_tip: Enter performance charts with PA and temp, then correct for weight, wind, slope, and surface. Always be conservative — round against you.
key_facts: Use POH/flight manual performance charts
Enter with pressure altitude and temperature
Apply corrections: weight, wind, slope, surface
TODR = ground roll + distance to 50 ft screen height
Always use conservative figures
Round PA and temp up, headwind down
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6 (performance charts), AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.102.2

syllabus_desc: Explain the difference between take-off distance required (TODR) and take-off distance available (TODA)
concept: TODR is the distance the aircraft needs to accelerate from a standing start, become airborne, and climb to 50 ft above the runway surface, under given conditions (weight, altitude, temperature, wind, slope, surface). TODA is the physical distance available for takeoff — it equals the length of the takeoff run available (TORA) plus any clearway beyond the runway end. For a safe takeoff, TODA must be greater than or equal to TODR. If TODR exceeds TODA, the takeoff cannot be made safely and the pilot must reduce weight, wait for better conditions, or use a longer runway.
exam_tip: TODR = what you need. TODA = what you have. TODA must be ≥ TODR or don't go.
key_facts: TODR: distance required to take off and clear 50 ft
TODA: distance available (TORA + clearway)
TODA must be ≥ TODR for safe takeoff
TORA: runway length available for takeoff roll
Clearway: obstacle-free area beyond runway end
If TODR > TODA, reduce weight or wait for better conditions
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.102.4

syllabus_desc: Given typical performance data, demonstrate the ability to determine LDR
concept: Using the POH landing performance charts, determine the Landing Distance Required (LDR). Enter the chart with pressure altitude and temperature, then apply corrections for aircraft weight, headwind/tailwind component, runway slope, and surface condition. LDR includes the distance from crossing the runway threshold at 50 ft to a full stop. As with takeoff charts, always be conservative. Landing performance is often more variable than takeoff due to pilot technique — actual landing distance may exceed the published figures.
exam_tip: Enter landing charts with PA and temp. Correct for weight, wind, slope, surface. LDR = from 50 ft over threshold to full stop.
key_facts: Use POH landing performance charts
Enter with pressure altitude and temperature
Apply corrections for weight, wind, slope, surface
LDR = distance from 50 ft threshold to full stop
Actual distances often exceed published figures
Be conservative — pilot technique affects landing distance
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6 (performance), AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.104.2

syllabus_desc: Explain the difference between landing distance required (LDR) and landing distance available (LDA)
concept: LDR is the total distance the aircraft needs to cross the runway threshold at 50 ft, touch down, and decelerate to a complete stop under given conditions. LDA is the physical length of runway available for landing — measured from the threshold to the end of the runway (or to any displaced threshold at the far end). For a safe landing, LDA must be greater than or equal to LDR. If LDR exceeds LDA, the pilot must divert to an aerodrome with a longer runway, or wait for more favourable conditions (e.g. stronger headwind).
exam_tip: LDR = what you need to stop. LDA = what you have to stop in. LDA must be ≥ LDR.
key_facts: LDR: distance from 50 ft threshold to full stop
LDA: physical runway length available for landing
LDA must be ≥ LDR for safe landing
If LDR > LDA, divert or wait for better conditions
Displaced threshold reduces LDA
Wet or slippery surfaces increase LDR significantly
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.104.4

topic: 12.106 — Definitions, Terminology and Abbreviations (Weight and Balance)
syllabus_desc: Arm (moment arm) definition
concept: Arm is the horizontal distance from the datum (reference point) to the centre of gravity of a weight or component. Arm is measured in inches or centimetres along the fuselage longitudinal axis. It determines the moment (turning effect) produced by a weight.
exam_tip: Arm = distance from datum. Measured in inches or cm. Formula: Moment = Weight × Arm. Positive arm is forward of datum, negative arm is aft of datum (in some systems). Always use consistent units when calculating moments.
key_facts: Arm is the horizontal distance from datum to a weight's centre of gravity
Arm is measured in inches or centimetres
Arm can be positive (forward of datum) or negative (aft of datum)
Moment = Weight × Arm
Different aircraft components have different arms listed in loading schedules
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

topic: 12.106 — Definitions, Terminology and Abbreviations (Weight and Balance)
syllabus_desc: Datum definition
concept: Datum is the reference point from which all arms are measured for weight and balance calculations. It is a fixed point defined for each aircraft type, typically located near the nose or a point on the firewall. All horizontal distances for CG calculations are measured from the datum.
exam_tip: Datum is the zero reference point. Different aircraft types have different datum locations. The datum location is specified in the aircraft's weight and balance manual. All arm measurements are relative to datum. Example: 'Datum is located 78 inches aft of the aircraft nose.'
key_facts: Datum is a fixed, aircraft-specific reference point
Datum location is defined in the weight and balance manual
All arms are measured from the datum
Datum is typically at or near the nose or firewall
Datum location is constant for a given aircraft type
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

topic: 12.106 — Definitions, Terminology and Abbreviations (Weight and Balance)
syllabus_desc: Moment definition and units
concept: Moment is the turning effect or rotational force produced by a weight at a given distance (arm) from the datum. Calculated as Moment = Weight × Arm. Units are typically pound-inches (lb-in), kilogram-centimetres (kg-cm), or Newton-metres (N-m). Total aircraft moment is the sum of all individual component moments.
exam_tip: Moment = Weight × Arm. If weight is in pounds and arm is in inches, moment is in pound-inches (lb-in). Always check unit consistency. For balance calculations, total aircraft moment divided by total weight gives the CG position.
key_facts: Moment = Weight × Arm
Units: typically lb-in, kg-cm, or N-m depending on weight and arm units
Positive moment = force causing nose-up rotation (forward of datum)
Negative moment = force causing nose-down rotation (aft of datum)
Total aircraft moment = sum of all component moments
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

topic: 12.106 — Definitions, Terminology and Abbreviations (Weight and Balance)
syllabus_desc: Centre of Gravity (C of G) definition
concept: Centre of Gravity is the point at which the entire weight of the aircraft can be considered to act. It is the balance point where the sum of all moments equals zero. C of G is calculated as: C of G arm = Total moment ÷ Total weight. The C of G location must remain within the C of G envelope for safe flight.
exam_tip: CG = point of balance. Calculate: CG arm (inches) = Total moment (lb-in) ÷ Total weight (lb). If calculated CG is outside the C of G range limits, the aircraft is out of balance and unairworthy. CG position affects stability and control.
key_facts: C of G is the balance point of the aircraft
C of G arm = Total moment ÷ Total weight
Units: typically inches from datum
C of G must remain within limits defined in aircraft manual
Forward C of G = more stable, requires more tail force, longer takeoff
Aft C of G = less stable, requires less tail force, shorter takeoff
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

topic: 12.106 — Definitions, Terminology and Abbreviations (Weight and Balance)
syllabus_desc: Longitudinal C of G range and associated limits
concept: Longitudinal C of G range is the permissible distance (in inches from datum) within which the aircraft's centre of gravity must lie for safe operation. Defined by the aircraft manufacturer, it includes a forward limit (usually the aerodynamically most forward safe position) and an aft limit (usually the least stable safe position). Operating outside this range is prohibited.
exam_tip: The C of G range is specific to each aircraft type and is found in the weight and balance manual. Example: 'Forward limit: 88.0 inches aft of datum; Aft limit: 94.0 inches aft of datum.' Always verify calculated CG is within this range before flight. Being outside the range affects controllability and stability.
key_facts: C of G range is defined by manufacturer for each aircraft type
Forward limit = most forward safe CG position
Aft limit = most aft safe CG position
Operating outside range: aircraft is unairworthy and unsafe
Range is typically expressed as two values: forward and aft limits
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

topic: 12.106 — Definitions, Terminology and Abbreviations (Weight and Balance)
syllabus_desc: Station definition
concept: Station is a longitudinal reference point on the aircraft expressed as a distance (in inches or centimetres) from the datum. Stations mark the locations of major aircraft components and are used to identify component positions for weight and balance calculations. Each major component (cockpit, cabin, engine, tail) is assigned a station.
exam_tip: Stations are like distance markers along the aircraft. Example: 'Pilot station = 35 inches aft of datum; Fuel tank = 96 inches aft of datum.' Stations are listed in loading schedules and manuals. You use station numbers to identify component arms for moment calculations.
key_facts: Station is a defined point at a specific distance from datum
Measured in inches or centimetres along the fuselage
Stations identify locations of aircraft components
Major stations: engine(s), cockpit, cabin, fuel tanks, tail
Loading schedules reference station numbers for arm values
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

topic: 12.106 — Definitions, Terminology and Abbreviations (Weight and Balance)
syllabus_desc: Basic aircraft empty weight (standard empty weight) definition
concept: Basic aircraft empty weight (also called standard empty weight, or SEW) is the weight of the aircraft structure, engines, all permanently installed equipment, unusable fuel, and all operational fluids (oil, hydraulic, coolant) but excluding crew, usable fuel, and cargo. It includes the manufacturer's standard equipment package and is used as the baseline for weight and balance calculations.
exam_tip: BEW (or SEW) = aircraft + engines + all fixed equipment + unusable fuel + oils, but NO crew, NO fuel, NO cargo. This is the starting point for calculating loaded weight. Verify the BEW value in the aircraft's weight and balance report and use it as the baseline.
key_facts: Basic empty weight (BEW) includes aircraft structure and engines
BEW includes all permanently installed equipment
BEW includes unusable fuel and operational fluids (oil, hydraulic, coolant)
BEW excludes crew, usable fuel, and cargo
BEW is the baseline for calculating loaded weight
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

topic: 12.106 — Definitions, Terminology and Abbreviations (Weight and Balance)
syllabus_desc: Zero Fuel Weight (ZFW) definition
concept: Zero Fuel Weight (ZFW) is the total weight of the aircraft including crew, passengers, and cargo, but excluding all usable fuel. It is calculated as: ZFW = Basic Empty Weight + Crew + Payload (passengers, cargo, baggage). ZFW is used to determine fuel-loading limits and is a reference point in weight and balance calculations.
exam_tip: ZFW = aircraft empty weight + crew + cargo, but NO fuel. ZFW is important for determining how much fuel can be added without exceeding maximum gross weight. Formula: Maximum fuel load = Maximum Gross Weight − ZFW.
key_facts: ZFW = Basic Empty Weight + Crew + Cargo + Baggage
ZFW excludes all usable fuel
ZFW is used to determine fuel-loading limits
Maximum fuel load = Gross Weight Limit − ZFW
ZFW must be within aircraft limits for each loading configuration
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

topic: 12.106 — Definitions, Terminology and Abbreviations (Weight and Balance)
syllabus_desc: Gross Weight (AUW - All Up Weight) definition
concept: Gross Weight, also called All Up Weight (AUW) or Maximum Takeoff Weight (MTOW), is the total weight of the aircraft including structure, engines, crew, fuel, passengers, cargo, and all other items. It represents the maximum weight at which the aircraft is authorized to operate. Operating above this weight is prohibited.
exam_tip: Gross Weight = BEW + Fuel + Crew + Cargo. This is the total weight at takeoff. Never exceed the aircraft's maximum certified gross weight. Verify that calculated loaded weight is within limits before flight.
key_facts: Gross Weight = Total weight of everything on aircraft
Includes: structure, engines, crew, fuel, cargo, equipment
Also called AUW (All Up Weight) or Maximum Takeoff Weight (MTOW)
Operating above gross weight limit is prohibited and dangerous
Gross weight affects performance and controllability
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

topic: 12.106 — Definitions, Terminology and Abbreviations (Weight and Balance)
syllabus_desc: Maximum Certified Takeoff Weight (MCTOW) definition
concept: Maximum Certified Takeoff Weight (MCTOW) is the maximum weight at which the aircraft is certified and permitted by the manufacturer and regulatory authorities to takeoff. It is defined by structural limits, aerodynamic limits, and performance requirements. Operating above MCTOW is prohibited and renders the aircraft unairworthy.
exam_tip: MCTOW is the absolute maximum weight for takeoff, set by the manufacturer and enforced by regulations. Different from MTOW (which may vary with configuration). Always check aircraft weight and balance manual for MCTOW and ensure loaded weight does not exceed it.
key_facts: MCTOW is the manufacturer-certified maximum takeoff weight
Set by regulatory certification process
Represents limit of structural and aerodynamic certification
Operating above MCTOW is illegal and unsafe
Different from AUW or Gross Weight (which may be lower)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: Calculate the C of G position given basic aircraft load sheet data
concept: C of G position is calculated by determining the total moment of all aircraft components and dividing by total weight. Process: (1) List all items with weight and arm, (2) Calculate moment for each item (weight × arm), (3) Sum all moments to get total moment, (4) Divide total moment by total weight to get C of G arm position.
exam_tip: Step-by-step method: Create a table with columns for Item, Weight (lbs), Arm (inches), and Moment (lb-in). Multiply each weight by its arm to get moment. Sum the weight column and moment column. CG = Total Moment ÷ Total Weight. Result is in inches from datum. Verify it's within limits.
key_facts: C of G arm = Total moment ÷ Total weight
Each component has weight and arm listed in loading sheet
Moment = Weight × Arm
Total moment = sum of all component moments
Result must be within aircraft C of G limits
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: Use a typical loading graph to determine C of G position
concept: Loading graphs (also called weight and balance graphs or envelopes) are graphical tools that allow rapid determination of C of G without calculation. The graph has axes: one axis shows weight or zero fuel weight (ZFW), the other shows moment. Plot the total weight and total moment as a point on the graph. If the point falls within the shaded envelope, the aircraft is in balance.
exam_tip: To use a loading graph: (1) Calculate total weight and total moment using load sheet data, (2) Find the total weight on the vertical (or horizontal) axis, (3) Follow to the corresponding moment, (4) Plot the point (weight vs moment), (5) If point is within the shaded envelope, CG is acceptable. Read CG position from the envelope boundary or reference lines.
key_facts: Loading graph eliminates need for division calculation
