Axes are typically Weight vs Moment
Shaded envelope shows acceptable CG range
Point outside envelope = unacceptable C of G
Graph method is faster than calculation method
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: Use index units to determine C of G position given a basic aircraft load sheet
concept: Index units are a simplified weight and balance system that eliminates large numbers. Instead of calculating moments directly, weight is multiplied by an index factor (usually 100 or 1000) to get index units. Total index = sum of all component indices. C of G arm = (Total index ÷ Index factor) ÷ Total weight. This method reduces calculation errors and uses smaller numbers.
exam_tip: Index system: (1) Each component weight is multiplied by its index factor to get index units, (2) Sum all index units, (3) C of G arm = (Total index ÷ Index factor) ÷ Total weight, or read directly from index graph. Example: If index factor is 100 and total index is 8,500 and weight is 2,500 lbs, then CG = (8,500 ÷ 100) ÷ 2,500 = 0.034, which converts to actual arm distance.
key_facts: Index units simplify weight and balance calculation
Index = Weight × Index factor (typically 100 or 1000)
Reduces decimal places and large moment numbers
Maintains same accuracy as moment calculation
Some aircraft use index graphs for direct CG determination
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: State the reasons for operating an aircraft within the C of G limits
concept: The aircraft must be operated within its published C of G limits to ensure safe and controllable flight. If the C of G is outside these limits, the aircraft may become difficult or impossible to control. The C of G limits are set by the manufacturer based on structural strength, stability requirements, and control authority. Flying within limits ensures: adequate longitudinal stability (the aircraft tends to return to trimmed attitude after a disturbance), sufficient control authority (the elevator/stabilator can maintain control throughout the flight envelope), structural integrity (loads remain within design limits), and predictable stall characteristics.
exam_tip: C of G within limits = stable, controllable, structurally safe. Outside limits = potentially uncontrollable.
key_facts: C of G limits set by manufacturer
Ensures adequate longitudinal stability
Ensures sufficient elevator control authority
Maintains structural integrity
Ensures predictable stall and spin characteristics
Flying outside limits may make aircraft uncontrollable
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6, AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.110.2

syllabus_desc: Forward C of G limit: effect on stability and control
concept: The forward C of G limit defines the most forward position the centre of gravity can be located. Operating at or near the forward limit: (1) Increases aerodynamic stability (aircraft resists pitch changes), (2) Increases control forces needed (heavier elevator control, more difficult to pitch up), (3) Increases takeoff and landing distances (tail down force needed to balance), (4) Reduces tendency to stall (more stable).
exam_tip: Forward CG = STABLE but DIFFICULT TO CONTROL. If CG is at forward limit: aircraft is very stable (good), but you need more control force to pitch up (bad), and takeoff run is longer. The tail-down force required increases, reducing available lift for climb. You won't stall as easily, but you need more stick force to achieve pitch.
key_facts: Forward CG limit is the most forward safe position
Forward CG increases aerodynamic stability
Forward CG increases elevator control forces required
Forward CG increases takeoff distance (tail-down force needed)
Forward CG increases landing distance
Forward CG reduces stall tendency (more stable margin)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: Aft C of G limit: effect on stability and control
concept: The aft C of G limit defines the most rearward position the centre of gravity can be located. Operating at or near the aft limit: (1) Decreases aerodynamic stability (aircraft is sensitive to pitch changes), (2) Decreases control forces needed (lighter, more responsive controls), (3) Decreases takeoff and landing distances (less tail-down force needed), (4) Increases stall tendency (reduces stall margin, aircraft can pitch up more easily).
exam_tip: Aft CG = UNSTABLE but EASY TO CONTROL. If CG is at aft limit: aircraft is difficult to control (sensitive, may oscillate), controls are light (good for control responsiveness), stall margin is reduced (risky), but takeoff/landing distances are shorter. The aircraft becomes increasingly difficult to control safely as CG moves aft. Never exceed aft limit.
key_facts: Aft CG limit is the most rearward safe position
Aft CG decreases aerodynamic stability
Aft CG decreases elevator control forces required
Aft CG decreases takeoff distance
Aft CG decreases landing distance
Aft CG reduces stall margin (increases stall danger)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, AC 61-3 Rev 25
